responseSet.title: Autistic Devices in Small Children in Mourning
responseSet.authorMast: Mario Gomberoff and  Liliana Pualuan de Gomberoff
responseSet.authorCitation: Gomberoff, M. & de Gomberoff, L. P.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autistic Devices in Small Children in Mourning

Mario Gomberoff and Liliana de Gomberoff

Liliana de Gomberoff

Dr M. Gomberoff
Pedro Blanquier 5572
Las Condes
Santiago
Chile

Liliana de Gomberoff  

The authors set out to show what happens to two small girl-patients who, starting from similar events (mother's pregnancy and birth of a sibling), developed traumatic mourning that could not be managed in the way that is usual for normal or neurotic children. Autistic devices were generated in these patients in order to protect their mental economy. They consider that this way of reacting is not justified either by the quality or by the intensity of the external stimuli and that these patients may have had a predisposition. To operate with this mechanism, the patients had to distort their mental apparatus and their own self in such a way that expressions such as language were affected in both of them: verbal language disappeared in one of them and in the other fulfilled functions other than communication. Atrophies and breaks in development to impede perception and meaning of the facts from becoming loose were also generated, strengthening the denial. The analyst's protective inclusion and his sustained effort to understand what was happening, to maintain communication and a receptive and understanding attitude, plus interpretation, made it possible for both patients to rely on a new device, constituted by the analyst, who helped to lessen the hard protective layer and make sense organs and self more permeable, processing the conflict so that more normal development could resume.

Corrections

‘Tender love and transference: unpublished letters of C. G. Jung and Sabina Spielrein’, Volume 80, Part 6: 1189-1204 by Zvi Lothane: in the acknowledgement on page 1202 it should have been made clear that permission for publication of Jung material rests with the Jung Estate and we would like to express our sincerest apologies to the Jung Estate for this error and for any misunderstanding that may have been caused.

Reviewers for 1999, Volume 80, Part 6: 1279-80: in addition to those listed we would like to express our thanks to the following reviewers for the North American Board and apologise for their omission from the original list: Charles Hanly, Alice Jones, Nathan Kravis, Jean Roiphe.

Cassandra's Daughter: A History of Psychoanalysis in Europe and America by Joseph Schwartz, reviewed by David Tuckett, Volume 80, Part 6: 1247-50: the reference to Khan 1974 on page 1248, column 2, lines 1-2, should have stated ‘on Freud and Montaigne’, not Freud and Montesquieu.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Teoría psicoanalítica. Su doble eje central: la tópica psíquica y la dinámica pulsional [Psychoanalytic theory. Its dual focus: psychic topography and drive dynamics]

José Gutierrez Terrazas. 1998. Madrid: APM Biblioteca Nueva. Pp. 206.

Pedro Nácher and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

C. Duque de Calabria 8-1a
46005 Valencia

((Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI, Cantab.)

Philip Slotkin  

Gutierrez Terrazas's book invites us to revisit and reflect anew on Freud's metapsychology from two different standpoints, namely topography (the formations of the unconscious) and dynamics (drives—in particular, sexuality). Freud proposed ‘that when we have succeeded in describing a psychical process in its dynamic, topographical and economic aspects, we should speak of it as a

838

metapsychological presentation’ (1915, p. 181). We immediately notice the absence, in the author's exposition, of Freud's third element, the economic aspect, which relates to ‘sums of excitation’. A close reading of the book reveals the reasons for this omission. It is not inadvertent, but conforms to the theoretical position of the author, who subscribes to the singular hypotheses of Laplanche, one of the main exponents of what has come to be known as the ‘French school of psychoanalysis’. This school is of course by no means homogeneous, but includes different tendencies that give rise to different readings and exegeses of the Freudian corpus (for the sake of illustration only, I would mention Green as an example of a different view, at variance with some of Laplanche's propositions).

The author seeks to distinguish the subject matter of psychoanalysis from that of academic psychology, which is dominated in our universities by behaviourism, as well as, in the last few decades, cognitivism. Psychology, he tells us, is devoted to the study of the adaptive order, whereas psychoanalysis is concerned with the affective or fantasy order. The main contribution of psychoanalysis, and also its field of exploration, is the ‘analytic situation’, which is defined by the following three elements: (a) its rules (‘say everything’ and ‘listen to everything on one and the same level’); (b) its method of ‘free association’; and (c) the transference—countertransference relationship. The unconscious and the drives are deemed to be the pillars of the ‘psychic reality’ discovered by psychoanalysis, and listening to the unconscious the objective of our clinical practice.

[[RunningHead]]

La psicoanalisi come letteratura e terapia [Psychoanalysis as literatur and therapy]

Antonino Ferro. Milan: Raffaello Cortina Editor. 1999. Pp. 176

Andreina Robutti

Andreina Robutti

Via Monte Rosa 15, 20149 Milan
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Andreina Robutti  

it is not ‘the mind’ that governs the instincts … the human problem is having a mind … It is the existence of a mind that has been unable to develop that gives rise to antisocial and violent behaviour … the violence is not in the instinct… it is a suffering mind that disturbs … if man did not have a mind, he would be a functioning primate (p. 115; my italics).

These ideas, which stem from Bion, underlie the entire psychoanalytic research of Antonino Ferro, who has devoted himself for years to the subject of the encounter between two minds in the analytic session and its transformational potential. This is his third book, after The Bipersonal Field: Experiences in Child Analysis (published in Italy in 1992 and in English translation in 1998) and Nella stanza d'analisi [In the analyst's consulting room] (1996).

What does Ferro see as a malfunctioning mind? It is a mind cluttered with beta elements that cannot be metabolised nor digested by a deficient alpha function, which give rise to the suffering of unthinkability and must therefore somehow be evacuated.

The crux of the matter is therefore not so much the accumulation of beta elements as the deficiency of the alpha function—i.e. damage due to the very early failure of ‘social’ relations, which has prevented introjection of the alpha function, the fundamental component of any form of ‘mentality’ (p. 3).

According to this conception of the mind, the whole of psychopathology (from panic attacks via hypochondria, phobias and hallucinations to delusions and other such manifestations) can be revisited in terms not of the symptom but of the genesis of the disturbance. This is placed in the relational context of the analysis, where ‘mental facts that have never happened before can come to pass, constituting the “big bang” that kindles the mental processes, which depends on the existence of a relationship with another mind’ to collect the evacuated beta elements and transform them into alpha—thus eventually permitting ‘the establishment of an autonomous alpha function’ (p. 5).

[[RunningHead]]

Féminin et féminité [Femininity and the feminine]

Monique Cournut-Janin. Paris: Presses Univ. France, Collection Epîtres. 1998. Pp. 173.

Hélène Troisier

Hélène Troisier

51 boulevard Beauséjour, 75016 Paris
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Hélène Troisier  

Monique Cournut-Janin had already published a large number of contributions on female psychosexuality before writing this book. The majority of the threads of her research are here drawn together in a rich and clearly presented volume. With particular reference to Freud's texts and on the basis of her many years of clinical experience, the author sets out her views on the development of the female psyche: ‘The model of neurosis, with the Oedipus and castration complexes as its referents, is the preferential organiser of the issues addressed’(p. 14).

Cournut-Janin points out that a man who writes about women is placing himself ‘on the other side of his sex’(p. 24). This was the case when Freud developed his ideas on female psychosexuality. She acknowledges that her own reading of Freud's texts on this subject was also undertaken from a vantage-point ‘on the other side of her sex’.

The author reminds us that fantasy activity—the infantile theory of sexuality—underlay the entry of the castration complex into Freudian theory. Starting from the phallic organisation of the little boy, Freud extended the organising fantasy to both sexes. The attribution of a penis to all human beings and its absence in women would be the nuclei for the development of castration anxiety and its oedipal effects in both girls and boys. The entirety of Cournut-Janin's interesting project is directed towards demonstrating the differences aroused by castration anxiety in both sexes. In a rigorously argued exposition, she guides us along the pathways of femininity, discussing each of the stumbling blocks that punctuate them. In her view, ‘certain mechanisms involved in the organisation of femininity are a response to the paternal fantasy’(p. 17).

In explanation, she adduces the image of the mother as the ‘messenger of castration’, but with a distinctly personal note. Upon the birth of a child, the mother, whose psychic life is organised by her own castration complex, finds her oedipal guilt reactivated by the proximity of the real child to the child of her oedipal desire. ‘This mother thus transmits to the child of either sex both desire and prohibition’(p. 69). In her unconscious she retains the traces of her baby-girl desires, including the ambivalent wish to take possession of all coveted objects, such as the father's penis. Having become the mother of a little girl, she projects on to her daughter her countercathectic mechanism, which might be translated as: ‘Your genitals must not seek to take in or incorporate your father's; you must not take pleasure from your father's genitals or risk destroying them’(p. 69). This anxiety about destroying the coveted object links up with the always dormant male castration anxiety (in the girl's father), which is ever ready to flare up when faced with the female genitals. Cournut-Janin notes that in Freud's writings it is the sight of the body of the opposite sex that reveals difference to the human subject. The observation of the lack of a penis and the wish to appropriate it will trigger psychic reorganisation in both boys and girls.

responseSet.title: Darwin Revisited: The Phylogeny Of Emotions
responseSet.authorMast: Juan-Francisco Jordan Moore and  José Barchilon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Darwin Revisited

The Phylogeny Of Emotions

Juan-Francisco Moore and José Barchilon

José Barchilon

78 Sutton Manor Road
New Rochelle
NY 10805

José Barchilon  

Theodore Shapiro and Waldemar Zusman delivered their papers but Gertie Bögel' was cancelled. After consultation with the presenters and the audience, the Moderator, Juan-Francisco Jordan Moore, decided to allot the extra time to the participants and to questions from the audience. We ended up with little time for questions from the floor.

Both Shapiro and Zusman documented the extent and use of Freud' knowledge of evolution theory. They referred to overlooked papers by Ritvo (e.g. 1974). But overlap was otherwise minimal, because they adopted different points of view for their talks. Shapiro posited that Freud very soon expanded Darwin theory in depth—he was not ‘bound to surface descriptive data’, he was studying the taxonomy of mankind mental processes. Shapiro divided his talk into two parts: (1) the evolutionary biology of attachment theory and (2) emotional expressions in relation to phylogenetic memory.

1) Evolutionary psychological biology started with Darwin, when he realised the importance of some form of language for survival of the species with small broods. The oviviparous precocial, as well as altricial, animals, require long, sometimes protracted period of care-taking by one or both parents or suitable substitutes. Their broods require an emotional expression of that attachment for calling their young, as well as responding in case of need or danger, a kind of language related to phylogenetic memory that is transmitted to the brood.

2) Freud made that idea essential to his theory of development and extended it to mental representations and memory, based on the model of the reflex arc—but becoming a sequence of signals of pain/pleasure and danger in the mind/brain, where they were internalised. Based on those mentalisations, in the absence of need-fulfilling objects, this leads to wish-fulfilling hallucinations, increasing the threshold for pain and frustration (Rapaport, 1950). It eventually led from action to thought, and thought as trial action. That process is paralleled by developmental mechanisms that allow passage from primal or primary-process types of expressions to secondary-process thinking and rational languages, and facilitate the passage from signs to symbolic languages and thoughts; as Shapiro put it: ‘the mechanisms of attachment are already in place prior to psychological and linguistic mechanisms that allow calling for mother by name’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Sixty-Two Interviews On ‘The Worst And The Best Episode Of Your Life’

Relationships Between Internal Working Models And A Grammatical Scale Of Subject-Object Affective Connections

Andrea Seganti , Giorgio Carnevale , Roberto Mucelli , Luigi Solano and Mary Target

Mary Target

Prof. Seganti
Via G. Ferrari 2
00197 Rome
London

Mary Target  

The authors address the issue of inferring unconscious internal working models of interaction through language. After reviewing Main's seminal work of linguistic assessment through the ‘adult attachment interview’, they stress the idea of adults' internal working models (IWMs) as information-processing devices, which give moment-to-moment sensory orientation in the face of any past or present, animate or inanimate object. They propose that a selective perception of the objects could match expected with actual influence of objects on the subject's self, through very simple ‘parallel-processed’ categories of internal objects. They further hypothesise that the isomorphism between internal working models of interaction and grammatical connections between subjects and objects within a clause could be a key to tracking positive and negative images of self and other during discourse. An experiment is reported applying the authors' ‘scale of subject/object affective connection’ to the narratives of sixty-two subjects asked to write about the ‘worst’ and ‘best’ episodes of their lives. Participants had previously been classified using Hazan & Shaver's self-reported ‘attachment types’ (avoidant, anxious and secure) categorising individuals' general expectations in relation to others. The findings were that the subject/object distribution of positive and negative experience, through verbs defined for this purpose as either performative or state verbs, did significantly differ between groups. In addition, different groups tended, during the best episodes, significantly to invert the trend of positive/negative subject/object distribution shown during the worst episode. Results are discussed in terms of a psychoanalytic theory of improvement through co-operative elaboration of negative relational issues.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Mental Handicap

Johan De Groef and Evelyn Heinemann. London: Free Association. 1999. Pp. 214.

Michael Kilchenstein

Michael Kilchenstein

1013 Boyce Avenue, Ruxton, MD 21204

Michael Kilchenstein  

To my knowledge, this collection of papers is the first organised work on the subject of the analytic treatment of the mentally handicapped. This book is a dramatic elaboration of the collective thinking and experience of a group of workers from seven different countries that met on two separate occasions to present and discuss their work. Both theoretical and clinical papers are included, and their underlying theme and mood is a homage to Maud Mannoni, who died

621

before the publication of the collection. She was a founder and the grande dame of the entire field of enquiry. The volume is justified by the information about Mannoni's work itself that the writers have made available. The clinical papers are wonderfully illustrative of a kind of freedom and adaptability that the workers have needed in order to help mentally handicapped people of all ages and with a wide spectrum of ailments. Nevertheless, a cognitive impairment is present in all of these patients, even though some of the writers clearly have come to the opinion that there is no way of clearly differentiating the physical and psychological origins of the deterioration of mental abilities. Although the writers come from various theoretical backgrounds, they advocate quite similar approaches in relating to and treating these patients and their families.

One of the authors’ most frequent messages is that each of the handicapped patients is first, and most fundamentally, fully a human being. Any attempt to reduce the individual to a nosological entity is likely to result in very limited and often disastrous outcomes. The approach most advocated is the creation of an environment wherein each individual can express his desires through his behaviour which the staff then interpret, and where the living situation is designed to make the best use of the individual's assets. The plans of the treatment span the entire gamut of psychotherapeutic possibilities, from the use of the entire village to a nearly classical analytic model. The explanations offered for the treatment approaches by different authors are given in the language of their paradigm (Freudian, Lacanian, Middle School, Kleinian etc.), but the observer sees patients being encouraged to pursue their own choices within a structure that is fairly conventional. As a sign of progress, patients very frequently show an increase in their ability to express themselves verbally, accompanied by a decrease in violent behaviour. Paradoxically, this improvement is often heralded by the onset of cursing.

responseSet.title: Motöverföring: Om omedveten kommunikation. [Countertransference: about unconscious communication]: Johan Norman and Franziska Ylander. Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. 1999. Pp. 212.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Motöverföring: Om omedveten kommunikation. [Countertransference: about unconscious communication]

Johan Norman and Franziska Ylander. Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. 1999. Pp. 212.

Henning Paikin

Henning Paikin

Kildevaenget 37, DK-2100 Copenhagen
henning@paikin.dk

Henning Paikin  

Eight experienced Swedish psychoanalysts have contributed to this anthology, the first part of which consists of seven clinical papers. They all demonstrate how the analyst's ‘dream-thinking’—such as fantasies, images, day-dreams—often meets the analysand's associations, although the analyst at first tends to dismiss his ‘dream-thinking as unrelated to the analysand's material’.

Franziska Ylander describes the transition between ‘recognizing oneself’ and ‘not recognizing oneself’ as an important part of the analyst's work with him-/herself. Anna Lena Isaksson and Agneta Sandell describe respectively a child and an adolescent analysis. Isaksson focuses on the analyst's opportunity of employing her own intrapsychic play together with her little analysand. Sandell, whose paper is based on one in Psychoanalysis in Europe (‘The adolescent process and psychic change’, 1998, Bulletin 50: 17-32) describes intensive provocation during a dramatic phase of an adolescent analysis and demonstrates how the transference and countertransference work with emotions of hate and violence could lead to a feeling of liberation. To give a more detailed impression of the book, three papers from the first section will be discussed in detail.

Clarence Crafoord's thesis in ‘Free association in the countertransference’ is that the analyst's consultation creates a meeting place for the analyst's and the analysand's psychic contents—images and thoughts appear and merge into a sort of synthesis.

Among Crafoord's vignette is a description of his disappointment when he realised that his former analyst—whom he had not seen for years—remembered neither him nor their joint analytic work. Soon afterwards, Crafoord himself did not recognise one of his own former analysands. These experiences make Crafoord reflect on whether the dreamworld of the analytic process may be so far from the everyday reality that it is quite natural that the analyst and analysand are estranged to each other when they incidentally meet in the reality outside the consulting room. Crafoord therefore views the analytic process as a dream.

responseSet.title: Cultural Expression of Affect
responseSet.authorMast: Moisés Lemlij and  Melvin R. Lansky
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cultural Expression of Affect

Moisés Lemlij and Melvin Lansky

Melvin Lansky

Lima

10921 Wilshire Blvd. Suite 509
Los Angeles
CA 90024
mlansky@ucla.edu

Melvin Lansky  

The first panellist, Judy Gammelgaard, presented a paper, ‘Catharsis or catastrophe’, which concerned a comparison of the cultural context of Greek tragedy with that of the cultural context of late twentieth century. Ritual and religion have lost their function as regulators of social life to modern people. Gammelgaard elaborated the function of tragedy as organised ritual purging. Modern festivals no longer serve such a function of harmonising and stabilising social life. The present-day dearth of such abreactive rituals leaves us with an uninterrupted steadiness of societal tensions and discontents about which we may wonder: are we headed towards catharsis or catastrophe? Freud's pessimism about the discontents of civilisation derived from his view of civilisation as requiring a build-up of aggression as satisfactions are increasingly given up for security. Segal has recently noted the regression to schizoid defences occasioned by the threat of massive destruction and threatening our capacity for empathy and compassion. Faced with the inevitability of fragility and decay and with the potential for massive destructive technology, our culture has no ritual outlet to enable mankind to reconcile itself to destructive forces. How are we to supply what ritual tragedy supplied for the Greeks?

A consideration of the nature of such a catharsis followed. Aristotle identified pity and terror as the distinctively tragic affects. Examination of these affects can help us further our enquiry. Terror presumes empathy; the protagonist's terror becomes our own. We share the hero's experience of threat. Such terror is also based on conditions that determine the course of events in the tragedy that link the protagonist's fate with ours. Pity, as an element that expresses the cruelties of the tragic predicament, also assumes identification and empathy with the protagonist. Gammelgaard proposes that we, as modern readers and theatregoers, might see the tragic affects of pity and terror as part of a process of mourning leading from the paranoid-schizoid to the depressive position. In this process originates our capacity for concern. If we are too identified with the hero, we feel terror, but not necessarily pity. Too much disengagement will not give us the aesthetic affects necessary for tragic catharsis. Pity is necessary for the expression of the cruelties of the tragedy, making possible a process of mourning that reconciles us to those cruelties. Pity and terror are not responses to cruelty. In Greek tragedy, experiences containing cruelty take place off-stage. Tragedy is about sorrow and grief, not cruelty per se. The form of tragic drama makes possible sublimated affective responses that help us to react with pity and terror to undeserved punishment.

On Feldman's ‘Some Views on the Manifestation of the Death Instinct in Clinical Work’

Richard Alexander

Richard Alexander

Richard Alexander  

Dear Sir

I am responding to Michael Feldman's article, ‘Some views on the manifestations of the death instinct in clinical work’ (IJP, 81: 53-65), which I found quite interesting but also a bit vexing, as it continues a trend in which clear distinctions between matters of physical survival and those having to do with the survival of ‘the life of the mind’ remain obscure.

responseSet.title: Work and the Evolving Self: Theoretical and Clinical Considerations: Steven D. Axelrod. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1999. Pp. 150.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Work and the Evolving Self: Theoretical and Clinical Considerations

Steven D. Axelrod. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1999. Pp. 150.

Jay Rohrlich

Jay Rohrlich

80 Wall Street, New York, NY 10005

Jay Rohrlich  

Work has been a relatively uninteresting subject for psychoanalysts. It is commonly seen as a necessary reality associated with survival, and not as a psychically determined phenomenon. In a footnote to Civilization and its Discontents, Freud said: ‘No other technique for the conduct of life attaches the individual so firmly to reality as laying an emphasis on work’. He allowed that a ‘large amount of libidinal components, whether narcissistic, aggressive, or even erotic’do get displaced ‘on to professional work and on to the human relations connected with it’, but also stated that ‘as a path to happiness, work is not highly prized by men. They do not strive after it as they do after other possibilities of satisfaction. The great majority of people only work under the stress of necessity’(1930, p. 80, fn. 1).

In this fine book, Axelrod makes a very convincing case for the fact that work is much more psychically determined than is generally believed, that intrapsychic representations of work are infinitely variable and subjective, and that these unconscious mental expressions provide a rich arena for psychoanalytic enquiry. Furthermore, in contrast to Freud's reluctance to see pleasure coming from work, Axelrod argues that ‘there is an irreducible capacity for mature pleasure in work life that is a touchstone of adult development’(p. 78). He refers to Jahoda, who suggested that ‘work provides an important means by which the pleasure and reality principles are synthesized. Work not only ties a person to reality, but offers the means by which he can obtain uniquely adult sources of pleasure’(1966, p. 628).

Axelrod firmly associates this ‘mature pleasure’with the vicissitudes of the aggressive drive, and the ego and identity gratifications included in Erikson's (1950) developmental schema. Work is a means by which we experience the aggressive satisfactions of mastery. Pride in our work products is central to a mature sense of self.

responseSet.title: Freud's Different Versions Of Forgetting ‘Signorelli’: Rhetoric And Repression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Different Versions Of Forgetting ‘Signorelli’

Rhetoric And Repression

Michael Billig

Michael Billig

Loughborough
LE11 3TU

Michael Billig  

The author examines Freud's three written accounts of his forgetting of the name ‘Signorelli’. The three texts—a letter to Fliess, an early scientific article and a chapter in ‘The Psychopathology of Everyday Life’—contain significant differences that are examined in detail. In describing the incident, Freud made successive changes that were matched to his theoretical explanations for the forgetting. More than this, close examination of the differences between the versions offers clues about Freud's own personal feelings that he was seeking to avoid exposing to his readers, while discussing the reasons for his slip of memory. It is suggested that Freud's thoughts are likely to have included his ambivalent feelings towards the death of his father, his sense of Jewishness and his relations with his sister-in-law, with whom he had been travelling when he had seen Signorelli's frescoes at Orvieto. Freud's other associations with Orvieto are examined. It is argued that the different versions of the episode represent a means by which Freud himself repressed uncomfortable thoughts exposed during his self-analysis. As such, this paper provides evidence for a discursive interpretation of repression.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dreams and Emotional Adaptation: A Clinical Notebook for Psychotherapists

Robert Langs. New York: Zeig, Tucker & Company, Inc., Arizona. 1999. Pp. 206.

Jerald Kay

Jerald Kay

Wright State University, Dayton OH 45401

Jerald Kay  

This is an especially timely book given that the teaching of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in US psychiatry training programs is under intense attack. The prolific Robert Langs, author or editor of over 40 books and 125 papers, has extended his earlier work based on frame and boundary concerns to address dream interpretation. Dreams and Emotional Adaptation is, in a sense, two separate books. More than the first half of this book is devoted to explaining the author's theory of emotional adaptation and how it is relevant to dreamwork. The remainder of the book describes the clinical application of this adaptive theory to understanding and interpreting dreams within the therapeutic relationship. Langs considers the dream to be the ‘very pinnacle of human communications’ (p. xi) and the mechanism by which we accomplish self-realisation, symptom alleviation and equanimity. Daily emotional events are triggers for dream formation, which in itself assists us in adapting to the challenges of our intrapsychic and interpersonal lives.

620

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman  

Dear Sir,

I read with great interest Imre Szecsödy's response to my paper, with which I am in full agreement.

The tendency—explored initially by Balint and Bernfeld, and more recently by Kernberg, Lussier and others—to push candidates into a passive position within a rigid training structure has been very much on my mind, and is more fully discussed in another paper of mine (2000—‘The utopian fantasy of a new person and the danger of a false analytic self’. Psychoanal.

1224

Psychol., 17: 38-60), also initially presented in Barcelona, in 1997.

I participated in the Pre-Congress on Training that Szecsödy describes, and found the format very productive. For example, some processes in the supervisor-supervisee dyad that presented in my discussion group could only be noticed by juxtaposing the supervisor's account and the supervisee's account, and I believe these processes would have escaped the group's attention completely had we heard only one or the other alone.

I therefore did not share the objections to the joint format raised mostly by British colleagues (most of whom avoided participating) and French colleagues (who came but expressed many concerns).

I felt, however, that there was something problematic in having groups composed of twelve to fourteen training analysts and one candidate (the supervisee in the presenting supervisor-supervisee dyad). My suggestion in the concluding discussion was to have in the future one joint day of the pre-congress on training and of the IPSO (candidates') conference, a day in which the discussion groups will be composed about equally of faculty members and of candidates (possibly not from the same institute).

This possibility has not materialised so far, but I do hope it will in the future.

Sincerely

July 2000

Emanuel Berman

responseSet.title: On James Herzog's ‘Blood and love’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On James Herzog's ‘Blood and love’

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro  

Dear Sir,

Having had the honour to be the discussant for Dr Herzog at Santiago (see Levinson, 2000), I am sending you a few of my reflections.

Dr Herzog's paper was about a child who ‘suffered mental abuse’ (Herzog, 2000). The abuse consisted in an inversion of the normal flow of projective identifications. The worried, suffering parents weren't able to provide their son with their mental power to accept and transform his anxieties. They didn't help metabolise his basic fears, and particularly they hadn't passed on to him a method of dealing with feelings and emotions. Indeed they did the opposite. They used the child to evacuate in him their own anxieties across the generation gap.

There is another story here. That of the analytic encounter, which has to bring in everything (so that, as Natalia would say, it can all be told). It has to come alive, and especially must be transformed

595

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower  

Dear Sir

Chabrol and Sztulman make two interesting points. They suggest that the transference-countertransference dynamic I described involved projective identification and that the transference reflected my patient's unconscious experience of incestuous pressure. I suspect that my different psychoanalytic lexicon may have left some unclarity regarding my understanding of the re-enactment dynamic, for I am largely in agreement with the writers on both points. My use of the term re-enactment assumed that processes of projective identification were operative; Emily unconsciously experienced aspects of the treatment relationship as a recreation of the maternal—child dynamic, responding to me as a sadistic, penetrating mother, and I identified with both my patient's experience and with her mother's. Within the analytic third (Ogden 1994), I contacted parallels to these issues in my own life and was able to indirectly use these with Emily in an effort to clarify a painful and dissociated dynamic.

The authors further suggest that adolescents may experience maternal intrusiveness as an incestuous menace, suggesting that my aversive countertransference involved a defense against incestuous erotic excitement. This is a crucial point, particularly in cases of sexual abuse. I agree (and thought I had made clear) that my patient's transference involved sexual (incestuous) elements along with more overtly sadistic ones. My intention was not to ignore the role of erotics in this transference-counter-transference pattern, but instead to underscore both the pre-oedipal nature of those erotics and their defensive functions.

I am unsure whether the authors' use of the term incestuous alludes particularly to oedipal level erotics; in my own view, my patient's erotic desire was more reflective of a pre-oedipal maternal erotic transference. The very messy, coercive and intrusive countertransference that I experienced is highly characteristic of the analyst's response to maternal erotic transference (Wrye & Welles, 1989). In recent years, my patient and I have traced the intertwined threads of maternal intrusiveness and abandonment to early childhood, suggesting that the origins of Emily's experience pre-date the adolescent memory through which we first accessed this dynamic.

responseSet.title: The Search For Meaning In The Affective Expressions Of The Adolescent Patient
responseSet.authorMast: Elizabeth Lima Da Rocha Barros and  Janice S. Lieberman
responseSet.authorCitation: Rocha Barros, E. D. & Lieberman, J. S.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Search For Meaning In The Affective Expressions Of The Adolescent Patient

Elizabeth Lima Rocha Barros and Janice Lieberman

Janice Lieberman

São Paulo

55 East 87th Street
New York
NY 10128

Janice Lieberman  

Peter Blos, Jr, the Co-ordinator, who put together this panel of experts on adolescence, briefly introduced the speakers and turned the meeting over to the Moderator. In a broad-based overview introducing the clinical work to be presented by two members of the panel, Elizabeth da Rocha Barros described this as an opportunity for analysts from different theoretical orientations, different countries, different languages and different clinical and conceptual languages to search for common ground and to understand differences in the ways meaning in mental life is conceived. The mind is organised around constellations of affects, or emotions, which give meaning to our experiences and psychoanalytic theory was built around the idea that mental organisations have meaning. Emotional situations, in order to become thinkable and communicable, therefore, must be transformed into symbols. Emotional experiences are not organised by chance, but are structured partly innately and partly as a result of life experiences. The analyst' task is to make mental representations of emotional situations visible and to rearticulate meanings from distinct symbolic spheres. This then opens up possibilities for new experiences and creates new meanings that expand the capacity for emotional development. Interpretations then should be both revealing and creative.

Rocha Barros then focused on the topic for this panel: the expression of affects in adolescence. She described the adolescent' need to separate from the family (no longer a container) and to work through once more oedipal rivalries with parents and siblings. She noted the oscillations in the adolescent resulting from the interplay between fantasies, defences and anxieties. There are moments of integration and disintegration, acting out and acting in, during the treatment of adolescents, producing strong countertransferential mental states in the analyst.

This served as an introduction to the complex and poignant case presented by Vincenzo Bonaminio, of a three-times-weekly analysis of a socially isolated and much-derided 16-year-old boy, Osvaldo, who came to analysis because of feeling plagued by anxieties about his sexual identity as well as his having homosexual fantasies. The very fragmented material was organised by Bonaminio into a coherent picture, as he focused in on the here-and-now interaction with his patient in the transference and countertransference. The boy lived in terror and shame about bodily sensations in the peri-anal area, which was found to be related to a fantasy of having a vagina near his anus. He felt different from others and did not know whether he was male, female or homosexual. The patient had to first trust the analyst, who was able to let himself ‘feel literally invaded by his dense, compact, machine-gun-fire’ talking. Osvaldo felt his inner state to be turbulent, then delusionally focused on the analyst' facial expression, all the while keeping eye contact and looking at Bonaminio' body. The analyst felt examined, scrutinised and controlled, a container of the unwanted feelings the patient was discharging. Through careful monitoring of the countertransference, Bonaminio was able to avoid making ‘all-knowing’ interpretations and so avoid repeating with the patient the role of omniscient mother from whom he was trying to escape (what adolescents call ‘detective moms’ in the US!). Bonaminio paid attention to the content of the patient' speech without linking it to their relationship. This technical adjustment decreased the pressure felt by the patient and a dialogue between them began to unfold.

responseSet.title: ‘Sketch for a Metapsychology of Affect’ by Iréne Matthis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Sketch for a Metapsychology of Affect’ by Iréne Matthis

Douglas Frayn

Douglas Frayn

315 Avenue Road, Suite 2
Toronto
M4V 2H2
doug.frayn@utoronto.ca

Douglas Frayn  

Iréne Matthis's paper appeared in Part 2, Volume 81, and was placed on the website for discussion on 26 April, 2000. The aim of this paper (presented at the 41st IPA Congress, Chile, 1999), was to transmit to the psychoanalytic reader Freud's two fundamental hypotheses as presented in An Outline of Psycho-Analysis (1940) and to present a contemporary sketch for the metapsychology of affect. She opens her paper by saying

It will not be easy reading, as not only will the task raise philosophical and epistemological issues with which psychoanalysts do not always feel at home, it will also ask the reader to adopt a perspective on conscious and unconscious processes that may initially feel somewhat counter-intuitive (p. 216).

I agree that this paper and the subsequent discussion during May and June 2000 were not ‘easy reading’but they gave psychoanalysts an opportunity to revisit controversies concerning earlier metapsychological formulations of affect and its unconscious processes. This is primarily a theoretical paper that cites neurophysiological research rather than a clinical one that puts forward psychoanalytic observations.

For Matthis, affect is a psychological concept lying on the frontier between the mental and the somatic. She finds support in Freud's second hypothesis in An Outline of Psycho-Analysis (1940), where he writes that psychology should look to the somatic processes to see the true essence of what is psychical. From this baseline the concepts of feeling, emotion and affect are differentiated and defined. Feelings and emotions are seen as two psychically perceived expressions of affective processes. The concept of affect is then defined by Matthis as an affective matrix linked to a predisposition of the bodily systems to react in a certain way to internal or external stimuli. The affective matrix can find expression either in psychically perceived affects—feelings and emotions—or in physically perceived ‘affect-equivalents’—psychosomatic symptoms and somatic illnesses. It is finally argued that this hypothesis finds support in the evolutionary perspective delineated by Freud in Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety (1926).

responseSet.title: A Stranger in my own Body: Domenico di Ceglie. 2000. Pp. 338.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Stranger in my own Body

Domenico di Ceglie. 2000. Pp. 338.

Egleé Laufer

Egleé Laufer

48 Abbey Gardens, London NW8 9AT

Egleé Laufer  

This book is based primarily on a selection of papers presented at a conference on gender identity development, held at the Tavistock Centre in London, in November 1996. It is edited by Domenico di Ceglie, the director of the Gender Development Unit at the Portman Centre, together with David Freedman. The title of the book most successfully evokes a sense of the unthinkable quality of the experience of children who are suffering from a gender identity development disorder, or, as termed by di Ceglie, as having an ‘atypical gender identity organization’. The fact that experience of such children or adolescents is at the core of this book is a reflection of how sensitively the issue has been treated. The contributions all come from professionals who are involved with the problem in different settings. Each of them brings a great depth of experience or research, which gradually allows the reader to appreciate the complexity of the issues related to the condition and its relation to the mental health of the child and adolescent.

In his own contribution to the conference, in a chapter entitled ‘Management and therapeutic aims’, di Ceglie makes a most important statement which clearly comes from his long clinical experience with children and adolescents brought to a gender clinic. He says: ‘Without the recognition and non-judgemental acceptance of the gender identity problem, no exploration can start’ (p. 186). Although this may seem something that psychoanalysts believe that they take for granted in their clinical work, di Ceglie is here warning us how difficult it is for anyone, parents, educators, doctors, as well as psychotherapists, not to fall into the trap of believing that it is a matter of a conscious choice made by the individual, even if we recognise that the motives for that choice are fundamentally unconscious. Freud said that the first thing we register when we see someone approaching us from a distance is whether they are male or female. Being confronted by someone whom we have identified as being male or female on the basis of their physical reality, but who then demands to be considered as if they are of the opposite gender, is experienced as an assault on one's reality testing. It is as if the most basic differentiation we are used to making can no longer be taken for granted. For children or adolescents to assert their right to be seen as consonant with their own psychic reality rather than with that shared through one's perception is easily confused with a sense that they are defying the authority of our shared reality, and, as such, doing something forbidden or violent. In his outline of the primary therapeutic aims in work with such children, di Ceglie shows the other areas of development that may be affected through their need to cling to psychic reality in defiance of the external reality. Thus although he adheres to the idea that we must not think in simple terms of pathology and cure, he is also saying that there are concomitant developmental problems that may arise as a result, which it is the therapist's task to address.

responseSet.title: Memory and therapeutic action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memory and therapeutic action

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes  

Dear Sir,

Peter Fonagy's Guest Editorial (‘Memory and therapeutic action’, Int. J. Psychoanal., 80:215-23) compellingly links the psychoanalytic tradition, current attachment literature, and cognitive ideas on the psychology of memory. From an attachment perspective it can be extended in a number of ways.

First, while the distinction between declarative and implicit memory is crucial, it is also important to consider what might be the relationship between these two memory systems. Attachment research suggests that secure and insecure developmental pathways tend to be relatively stable, so that children diagnosed as insecure in infancy will be similarly classified at school entry and subsequently. The mechanisms maintaining this stability are only just beginning to be discovered, but it presumably depends on consistent parent-child dyads in which each evokes a particular set of secure-or insecure-making responses from the other.

353

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affect And Development

Pablo Cuevas Corona and Nadine Levinson

Nadine Levinson

Mexico City

9 Breakers Isle
Dana Point
CA 92629

Nadine Levinson  

Pablo Cuevas Corona, the Moderator, opened the second day of the 41st IPA Congress panel on ‘Affect and development’ by introducing the main presenters: Robert Emde and James Herzog and the discussants: Rita Frankiel, Antonino Ferro and Romulo Lander. The Moderator was enthusiastic in his participation in this panel, which attempted to grapple with the complex issues around affect and development.

Because Robert Emde' research paper was pre-published in Volume 80 (1999), his presentation consisted of a review. He commented that psychoanalytic clinical thinking had broadened to embrace an organisational model of affect and the integrative influences of affective processes, beginning in early development. This view is informed both by psychoanalytic empirical research, clinical psychoanalysis and by multidisciplinary research. In Emde' opinion, this organisational, integrative view continues Freud' thinking around affects as signals and regulators. Throughout his presentation, he underscored the centrality of affects in object relations and its manifestations for analyst and analysand. Emde' main thesis proposed that emotions guide both aspects of developmental change and developmental continuity and creates developmental integration.

Emde proceeded to outline the six transitional periods that were elaborated in his pre-published paper. These phases are each examples of new and special affective signals demonstrating a new level of psychic organisation within the child and between the child and the family. He asserted that the self-enhancing functions, in contrast to self-maintaining and regulating functions of emotions, have been underemphasised.

In turning to his discussion of the affective core self concept, Emde explained that emotions and developmental continuity refer to a person having a consistent sense of self in the midst of change. Thus, the affective core self relies not only on biology, but also on continuous experience itself, which influences memory and procedural knowledge in non-conscious life. This affective core self is also linked to the regulating and background emotional influences with caretakers and others in the child' life.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

Dear Sir,

I very much appreciate Dr Migone's reminder that there is ‘little that is genuinely new under the sun’. He also appropriately, if gently, chides the author of the Guest Editorial for not acknowledging fully that in their important paper Migone and Liotti (1998) had advanced a comprehensive model of therapeutic action which included constructs quite similar to the ones suggested in the Editorial. I cannot even claim ignorance of the paper since, as Acting Internet Editor for the Journal, I was responsible for selecting their article for an exceptionally stimulating internet discussion.

In my defence, I would like to state that the aim of the Editorial was to spell out the implication of transference-oriented techniques (such as that recommended by Betty Joseph and her colleagues—see Joseph, 1985) for our model of the role of memory in therapeutic action. Thus connections between the model proposed in the Guest Editorial and similar models advanced by authors building on general systems theory-based approaches appeared to be less critical than establishing links between more traditional psychoanalytic approaches and recent research on memory. I can only add that, like Franz Alexander, I am Hungarian by birth, thus the possibility remains that the notion of recovered memories as consequences of therapeutic action rather than their cause was itself the recovery of a forgotten racial memory on my part.

Yours faithfully

February 2000

responseSet.title: The Role Of Affect In Motivation
responseSet.authorMast: Pedro Luzes and  Vivian B. Pender
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role Of Affect In Motivation

Pedro Luzes and Vivian Pender

Vivian Pender

Lisbon
The Montana, Suite 7F
247 West 87th Street
New York NY 10024

Vivian Pender  

Three papers, each with differing perspectives, were presented on the role of affect in motivation. Phyllis Tyson's paper emphasised current contributions from neuroscience. Florence Guignard explored the homeostatic function of affect and motivation in psychoanalytic treatment. Miguel Calmon du Pin e Almeida focused on the logic of psychoanalytic discourse as a motivator. Pedro Luzes, the Moderator, introduced the subject of the panel by reminding us that motivational systems are multi-determined and perform multiple functions. Freud's theory of affects, Luzes continued, presents us with two serious problems: affects that remain inside the body and love, which is considered a drive. Questioning whether Freudian concepts could be integrated with modern psychology, Luzes turned to the panel.

The first panellist, Phyllis Tyson, presented her paper, ‘Affect and motivation: how are they related?’ Tyson urged that we re-evaluate our basic psychoanalytic concepts using new scientific evidence. She began by reviewing affect and motivation in Freud's models of the mind. Early in Freud's theories, he concluded that affects were used to motivate protective, defensive behaviours. In 1895, he turned from neurophysiology to the principles of physics to understand brain functioning. Drive theory followed and affects were the discharge products of the drives. The structural model (1926) brought affects and ego functions together. As a signal of danger, affects could enhance adaptation or be traumatic.

In the second section of her paper, Tyson discussed contemporary challenges and the inherent contradiction in Freud's theory of signal anxiety. Since anxiety is both a danger signal and a driving force, anxiety motivates object-seeking as well as defensive behaviour. In this regard, signal theory led the way to other ideas about motivation such as biologically based attachment (Bowlby, 1960) or a wish for safety (Sandler, 1960) or for pleasurable experiences (Sandler & Sandler, 1978). Tyson objected to the subjection of more contemporary theories to ‘drive reductionism’, which has ‘resulted in a certain stagnation of the theoretical progress within psychoanalysis, a relative failure of psychoanalysis to develop scientific methodology in keeping some compatibility with other areas of science, and it has fostered a degree of isolationism from neighbouring disciplines’.

responseSet.title: Adolescence and Psychoanalysis: Maja Perret-Catipovic and Francois Ladame. London: Karnac Books. 1998. Pp. 186.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescence and Psychoanalysis

Maja Perret-Catipovic and Francois Ladame. London: Karnac Books. 1998. Pp. 186.

Luis De La Sierra

Luis De La Sierra

15 Highwood House, 148 New Cavendish Street
London W 1 M 7FH

Luis De La Sierra  

This is an overdue and welcome collection of essays dealing with one of the most neglected and fascinating themes in psychoanalytic literature: adolescence. The editors offer us a collection of carefully selected papers by classical and contemporary leading authorities in a field that has been fighting for recognition since the early days of the psychoanalytic movement. The contributions by S. Freud, A. Freud, Jeanne Lample-de Groot, Peter Blos, Evelyne Kestemberg and Moses Laufer have already been part of the classical reading in the field of adolescence and psychoanalysis. The articles by M. Perret-Catipovic and Francois Ladame, Philippe Gutton and Raymond Cahn are relatively recent. They offer interesting and up-to-date psychoanalytic views of the process of adolescence.

With an emphasis on the developmental approach so valued by Anna Freud and her followers, this is not a compendium of the psychopathology of adolescence, but a collection of essays invested in providing an insight into the vicissitudes and destiny of the normal adolescent eruption, as well as into the processes that may lead to later pathology, both during adolescence and in adulthood. After reading this important psychoanalytical contribution, I could not help remembering the words of Moses Laufer who, throughout the years (like Peter Blos), enthusiastically and passionately referred to this phase of development as ‘the second chance to put things right’.

1250

responseSet.title: Who Was Rumpelstiltskin?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who Was Rumpelstiltskin?

Harry Rand

Harry Rand

Washington DC
20560-0616

Harry Rand  

The author describes the way in which Rumpelstiltskin has perplexed and enthralled readers since the brothers Grimm recovered the tale from the realm of folklore. In standard translation, the story now lives in a fixed literary form and, consequently, in the imagination of every child who has ever heard or read the story—virtually every person in the English-speaking world. Therefore, deeply rooted in childhood experiences, Rumpelstiltskin can be expected to appear in analysis, and he does. The compelling central character is the title figure, Rumpelstiltskin, whose name and actions tell us who he is and what he was intended to ‘mean’—especially to his contemplated audience. The original narrators of and listeners to this tale were female visitors to the evening spinning chamber (Spinnstube), where women gathered and told tales to amuse themselves to ward off sleep while they spun. The butt of this story is male impotence and bluster, and the key to the story's meaning arises from matching the etymological roots of the central character's name with his actions as they appear philologically and psychoanalytically.

responseSet.title: Between Ourselves: Philosophical Conceptions of Intersubjectivity1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Between Ourselves

Philosophical Conceptions of Intersubjectivity1

Jeff Malpas

Jeff Malpas

GPO Box 452-41 Hobart
Tasmania 7001

Jeff Malpas  

The intersubjective is an essentially relational realm: it is the realm that lies between subjects—between ourselves (for we, of course, are among the subjects to whom the term refers)—and as it lies between, so it also represents the realm that is common to all, the realm of what is essentially public and open, the realm of communication and active engagement with others. Through our participation in such a realm we come to know something of how others feel, of what they believe, desire and hope, of who and what they are.

Yet within modern philosophy, in particular, our access to such a realm, and so our access to the feelings, beliefs, desires, hopes and identities of others, have both been viewed as questionable. The so-called ‘problem of other minds’ is just the problem of whether we do indeed have access to such intersubjectivity and, with it, to the subjectivity of others, as well as of the nature of that access. The problem at issue here is, of course, primarily epistemological—it concerns our knowledge of others and of their mental lives—but epistemological issues are not the only issues that arise in relation to intersubjectivity and neither is it only in relation to epistemological matters that intersubjectivity emerges as philosophically problematic. The very nature and possibility of intersubjectivity is itself open to question. To what extent, for instance, is intersubjectivity necessarily tied to language? To what extent is it tied to materiality and the body? What role might be played by the objective world in the structure of intersubjectivity? And might intersubjectivity already be implied in the very idea of subjectivity itself?

Inasmuch as the idea of the intersubjective brings a number of such questions with it, so there is not, within philosophy, any single, well-recognised or clearly defined ‘problem of intersubjectivity’ on which discussion can focus (something reflected in the absence of ‘intersubjectivity’ as a heading in most dictionaries and encyclopaedias of philosophy).

responseSet.title: Pleasure in Phantasy and Reality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pleasure in Phantasy and Reality

André Beetschen and Inge Wise

Inge Wise

Lyons

Pitt House, North End Avenue
London NW3 7HP
Inge@awise.demon.co.uk

Inge Wise  

Pleasure, fantasy and reality are—in the words of Roy Schafer—the three axes of clinical psychoanalytic work. The work of analysis is concerned with stabilising and sharpening the patient's sense of reality. It is equally concerned with finding a way for a creative and imaginative fantasy life to become established. In each of these areas primitive defences such as splitting, denial, idealisation, projective and introjective identification play a dominant and pathological role in limiting the patient's ability to experience pleasure.

Defining and analysing emerging unconscious phantasies about the self and others often bring in their wake greater understanding of our patients’ basic assumptions, past and present, which limit their ability to find satisfaction in love and work. Through the analysis of the transference, the emotional reality of childhood can be refound and integrated by the now adult patient.

In his clinical presentation ‘Pleasure in fantasy and reality’, Schafer chose material from the analysis of a middle-aged, unmarried male patient, Anthony, whose presenting problems included depression and severely restricted sexual and social enjoyment and relationships.

His parents had fled political persecution in a Mediterranean country after their parents and other close family members had been imprisoned, abused and killed. Their devastating losses left them bereft and emotionally unavailable to their son, who in turn left them and their chosen country of refuge to study and eventually pursue his career in the United States.

Their depression and sense of guilt at surviving not only marked and subdued their lives, but also deprived and narcissistically damaged their son, curtailing his abilities to find pleasure in reality and fantasy and to relate to others with excitement or with passion.

‘Borrowed guilt’, i.e. his parents’ survivor guilt introjected by Anthony, exacted a heavy price in terms of Anthony's mental health. His outwardly pleasing, intelligent, conscientious if somewhat life-less manner covered a sado-masochistic character structure. His manipulative relationships with women afforded him the opportunity to project his own neediness and vulnerability into them, while gaining a precarious sense of superiority from despising them for containing his own now disavowed feelings.

responseSet.title: Rage In The Analytic Setting
responseSet.authorMast: Henry J. Friedman and  Ana-Maria Rizzuto
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rage In The Analytic Setting

Henry Friedman and Ana-Maria Rizzuto

Ana-Maria Rizzuto

Cambridge, Ma
75 Gardner Road
Brookline, MA 02445

Ana-Maria Rizzuto  

The Moderator opened the discussion by reflecting on the conditions for and the need to experience affect in the clinical situation in order to lift pathogenic repressions. In the case of rage, he questioned whether it was always necessary for the patient to experience it fully, or whether the analyst should help the patient to grapple with the overt experience of very intense rage.

Stanley Coen focused on how analysts bear both their patient's rage and their own. He observed that in a 1991 panel of the American Psychoanalytic Association, entitled ‘Hate in the analytic setting’, each of the highly skilled analysts presenting their cases ‘seemed relieved as soon as his patient had moved away from hatred to some other feeling’. He referred to Merton Gill's stance at that meeting, where he suggested that analysts needed to tolerate and even enjoy being hated. Coen insisted that he took pride in being listed among the most hated analysts in the Americas. His pleasure relates to his conviction that to help patients, analysts must allow themselves fully to experience their feelings for their analysands and become aware of what they want from them. The early understanding of countertransference as pathological may have interfered with many analysts' ability to be open to their own inner needs while immersed in working with their patients. Many analysts are afraid of becoming aware of what they want from their patients as though this might pose an imminent risk of enacting their wishes and feelings. On the contrary, this risk is diminished when the analyst assumes internally full responsibility for his needs and wishes in relation to the analysand. The analyst's discomfort with the rage impedes the patient from fully expressing it and encourages a shift toward sado-masochism. Patients may comply with the analyst's discomfort with expressed rage out of fear of its effect on the analyst's vulnerability. Coen believes that analysts must tolerate their patients' rage and help them to own it as well as their hatred of the analyst. Patients cannot change significantly without feeling their rage and hatred deeply and strongly during analysis. The analyst cannot remain immune to this intense rage. He produces his own strong responses that need self-analytic attention and may require, in the case of chronically enraged patients, that the analyst seek help from colleagues to understand his/her own responses to persistent rageful attacks.

responseSet.title: Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy: Robert S. Wallerstein. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1998. Pp. 511.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy

Robert S. Wallerstein. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1998. Pp. 511.

Abby Adams-Silvan

Abby Adams-Silvan

300 Central Park West, New York, NY 10024

Abby Adams-Silvan  

Robert Wallerstein's Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy is an extraordinary and illuminating book about a potentially shattering crisis in the institutional history of psychoanalysis. It is also a testament to the capacity of an intimate witness to maintain a thoughtful and objective balance in reporting events that evoked the most passionate of arguments and the most virulent of accusations. This was because the controversy involved arose from fundamental splits within the professional community on an issue that resonates in our professional collective unconscious: what qualities and qualifications are required to be a psychoanalyst?

On 1 March 1985 an explosive landmark class action law-suit was filed in New York State by four PhD psychologists on behalf of 3000 members of the Division of Psychoanalysis of the American Psychological Association. The action named as Co-defendants the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA), the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA), and two New York City Institutes accredited by the American, the New York Psychoanalytic Institute and the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

The plaintiffs claimed to have been illegally denied access to quality training, and therefore to subsequent practice and remuneration in their profession of choice by the American Psychoanalytic Association because that organisation maintained an unfair and inappropriate monopoly based on a prerequisite medical degree for training and subsequent practice. The defendants were, therefore, accused of illegal ‘restraint of trade’.

606

Affect And Clinical Technique

Terttu De Folch and Jerome Kavka

Jerome Kavka

Barcelona

122 S. Michigan Avenue, 1311E
Chicago
IL 60603-6107

Jerome Kavka  

It is difficult to do justice to the richness of Green' original printed presentation, which had consisted of thirty-four densely packed pages in English, in which he gave evidence as a master clinician of broad experience as well as a diligent scholar of every aspect of the theoretical aspects of psychoanalysis including the subject of affects (see also ‘On discriminating and not discriminating between affect and representation’, 1999, Int. J. Psychoanal., 80:277-316). The paper he actually delivered departed somewhat from the printed version and is the one Ruth Stein attended to rigorously.

Green' presentation was entitled ‘On discriminating and not discriminating between affect and representation’. He began by describing his frame of mind at the beginning of an analytic session: he maintains free-floating attention, tries to perceive the patient' internal conflicts and ‘something addressed, implicitly or explicitly to me’, as he put it.

Despite his obvious respect for the work of Freud and his obvious mastery of Freud' complicated metapsychology, which he was reluctant to abandon in favour of new psychoanalytic theories that to him are often superficial, he nevertheless expressed his debt to Lacan, Bion and Winnicott in particular for his new frame of mind. As he put it, ‘what happens in the Freudian experience is not an observation of the visible but a reading of that emerging in an intersubjective, bi-personal field where what happens is co-determined by the agents present. The receiver becomes an inducer’, says Green. This is the core of the transference.

Because his presentation was cut short by the dramatic appearance and greetings from the President of Chile, Green was unable to present the most sensitive, detailed and dramatic last part of his paper. This was a comprehensive outline of the defensive limitations of borderline patients in which ‘denial of affect may reach extreme forms due to their inability to separate affects and representation’.

Andrade de Azevedo (São Paulo) opened her clinical presentation by saying that psychoanalysis deals with subjectivity and considers the intersubjective relationship as the central point of the psychoanalytic experience. Countertransference, which earlier was seen as a complication, is now regarded as a useful tool for working through.

responseSet.title: Developments in Infant Observation. The Tavistock Model: Susan Reid. London: Routledge. 1997. Pp. 232.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developments in Infant Observation. The Tavistock Model

Susan Reid. London: Routledge. 1997. Pp. 232.

Judith Jackson

Judith Jackson

35 Crooked Usage, London N3 3EU

Judith Jackson  

Ever since Esther Bick pioneered the introduction of infant observation into the training of child psychotherapists at the Tavistock Clinic in 1948, it has established itself as an invaluable tool for learning about the complexity and richness of the earliest relationships. This has now become an inbuilt module in most psychoanalytically based training, as well as in the training of allied professionals throughout many countries of the world. This book arose out of the first conference on infant observation, which took place at the Tavistock Clinic, London, in 1993. The conference aimed to explore and review the most significant theoretical developments that have taken place in infant observation since its conception, highlight the many research initiatives it has stimulated and examine its impact on clinical practice. The diversity of experience and creative thinking reflected in so many of the papers presented at this conference not only demanded to be heard by a wider audience—hence this book—but also fuelled the birth of a new journal on infant observation, underpinning the significance of ‘psycho-analytic’ infant-observation-based research and insights in the field of infant mental health.

Susan Reid has written an introductory chapter outlining the nature of infant observation for those readers unfamiliar with the task. She compares infant observation with looking at human relationships through a microscope, focusing on the tiny details involved in any human exchange. The observer is encouraged to be open-minded and supported ‘to see what is there to be seen and not to look for what they think should be there’ (p. 1). In this way, beginning an observation affords the student a rich and moving experience, while at the same time exposing him to a great deal of uncertainty and anxiety, stripping away omniscient and intellectual defences and stirring up his own unconscious infantile experiences. Throughout this chapter, and indeed the whole book, the reader is reminded of how the exercise of infant observation is a very different learning experience from one gained from reading texts on theories of child development. Wittenberg writes: ‘It is the opportunity to go back to the beginning of life that makes infant observation so worthwhile, stimulating us to get in touch with our own primitive anxieties, holding both terror and great potential for thought and emotional development’ (p. 26).

responseSet.title: On Slochower's ‘Erotic complications’
responseSet.authorMast: Henri Chabrol,  Henri Sztulman and  Dr Sztulman
responseSet.authorCitation: Chabrol, H., Sztulman, H. & Sztulman, D.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Slochower's ‘Erotic complications’

Henri Chabrol , Henri Sztulman and Dr Sztulman

Dr Sztulman

Dr Sztulman  

Dear Sir

We read with great interest Joyce Slochower's paper ‘Erotic complications’ (IJP, 80: 1119-30), reporting on an erotic transference, the countertransference of which led her to understand that it represented a reversed re-enactment of childhood and adolescence experiences that were not primarily sexual in nature. The mode of expression of that erotic transference evoked in the analyst feelings of intrusion, aversion, self-consciousness and coldness, which made her ask herself whether it was not a defence mechanism against erotic feelings. The main intervention was to reveal to her patient that she was feeling ‘put on the spot’ and that she asked herself if the patient had not experienced such a feeling when growing up. This intervention was followed a few weeks later by a new transference response: the patient felt self-conscious and scrutinised by the analyst, and recalled a forgotten ritual in which her mother scrutinised her body, searching for unsightly body hair and removing it. The erotic transference was considered to recreate in reverse an experience in which she had felt sadistically examined as an imperfect object and a potential source of humiliation for her mother. The primarily non-sexual nature of these adolescent experiences may be questioned: adolescents tend to feel intrusive parental attitudes towards their body as an incestuous menace. The transference-counter-transference dynamics might have reproduced both the sexual and non-sexual aspects of the relationship with her mother by a mechanism of projective identification: the patient seemed to identify with her mother's incestuous and sadistic intrusion and controlling attitudes and gave the analyst feelings of self-consciousness, humiliation, emotional penetration, helplessness or hostility that she had felt and repressed or from which she had reacted with isolation, denial or dissociation. She might also make the analyst experience the coldness with which she defended herself against the excitement and the desire to abandon herself to the intrusion of her mother.

responseSet.title: The Uninvited Guest: Emerging from Narcissism towards Marriage: James V. Fisher. London: Karnac Books. 1999. Pp. 304 + xxiii
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Uninvited Guest: Emerging from Narcissism towards Marriage

James V. Fisher. London: Karnac Books. 1999. Pp. 304 + xxiii

Jill Boswell

Jill Boswell

23 Jacksons Lane, London N6 5SR

Jill Boswell  

James Fisher's sub-title, Emerging from Narcissism towards Marriage, concisely expresses the main theme of his enquiry into the human struggle with intimate relationships. He is a psychoanalytically trained psychotherapist working in couple therapy at the Tavistock Institute of Marital Studies, which (founded as the Family Discussion Bureau in the 1950s) has long been at the forefront of marital work in Britain.

The theoretical underpinning of Fisher's clinical approach is quickly apparent. By ‘marriage’, he tells us, he means not matrimony as such, but a mental state in which the internal relationship to the object combines intimacy with the acknowledgement of separateness. It follows that a couple seeking and achieving separation may in objectrelations terms actually be moving towards the possibility of ‘marriage’, or ‘remarriage’. He suggests that ‘there is no good divorce that is not at the same time a marriage in the sense of a relationship that is not completely distorted by the narcissism of each partner, especially when there are children’ (p. 2).

Narcissism is defined ‘not as a pre-occupation and investment in the self as opposed to others, but as states of mind in which the reality of the other is attacked, undermined and denied’ (p. 2). For Fisher, it follows that the lessening of distortions caused by projective identification will enhance the ability of even a parting couple to manage the separation in a realistic and potentially healing way.

He links narcissism and marriage in the same way that Bion linked the paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions: narcissism ↔ marriage, thus helpfully showing the oscillation between the two states that will continue throughout life. Their interplay profoundly influences the structure and later vicissitudes of the Oedipus complex, and these two themes are interwoven through the book.

Fisher illustrates his themes first through literature: in the first and last chapters drawing on two Shakespeare plays, in the middle section on T. S. Eliot's seldom performed play, The Cocktail Party. At other points he offers clinical examples from his own work and, in an extended section, a detailed discussion of a conjoint therapy undertaken with a couple by two therapists in the Family Discussion Bureau, and published in Studies in Emotional Conflict and Growth (Pincus, 1960). Throughout, Fisher's theoretical discussion clearly reflects the influence of Klein and postKleinian writers in Britain, in particular the work of Bion and Meltzer.

responseSet.title: L'Enfant Et Les Sortilèges Revisited
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'Enfant Et Les Sortilèges Revisited11

Debbie Hindle

Debbie Hindle

13 Park Terrace
Glasgow G3 6BY

Debbie Hindle  

The author discusses ‘L'Enfant et les sortilèges’, an opera by Ravel based on a short story by Colette, which traces the trials and tribulations of a young boy whose bad behaviour leads to his being sent to his room, left alone and given only tea and bread until dinner. His progression from anger to persecution and fear, the various defences he employs to protect himself from feeling overwhelmed and his despair are graphically illustrated through words and music. The author considers the opera in relation to Klein's theory of the paranoidschizoid position and the struggle involved in maintaining contact with good objects, externally and internally. Revisiting the opera in light of Meltzer's contribution to psychoanalytic thinking provides a wider perspective in which to explore what he has termed the aesthetic conflict and its place in relation to the depressive position and developmental processes.

responseSet.title: Insight and the Nature of Therapeutic Action in the Psychoanalysis of 4- and 5-Year-Old Children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Insight and the Nature of Therapeutic Action in the Psychoanalysis of 4- and 5-Year-Old Children11

Angela Joyce and Jenny Stoker

Jenny Stoker

21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3 5SD

Jenny Stoker  

After a discussion of some of the theoretical issues raised in the past about the concept of insight, the authors go on to track changes in the internal representations of two young patients during the course of a session with each of them. Their argument is that in the attention to the detail of these sessions there is evidence of a gradual development of knowledge about internal relationships in the patient, which is reflected in movements in the narrative of the play. The need for congruence between heightened affect in the patient and the analyst conveying understanding is stressed and compared with early mother—infant relationships. They argue that the 5-year-old child can use his developing capacity to mentalise most safely in the context of pretend play and that the analyst's communications should remain as close as possible to that context, typically in displacement. They conclude that their sessions give evidence that children of this age are capable of a form of insight that could be said to be the equivalent of that of adults in analysis, but that their mode of expression and of communication of their self-knowledge through their play is very different.
