responseSet.title: The Primal Scene and Picasso's Guernica
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Primal Scene and Picasso's Guernica11

Raul Hartke

Raul Hartke

Rua Dona Laura 745, Apto. 301
Bairro Rio Branco
CEP 90430-091
Porto Alegre—RS
Brazil

Raul Hartke  

The author examines a group of works by Picasso dating from the late 1930s in terms of the artist's experiences as documented by his biographers and of primal-scene fantasies as described in the field of psychoanalysis by, in particular, Freud and Klein. Pointing out that the artist himself is on record as inviting such a consideration, he contends that these fantasies constitute the latent motivating force behind one of Picasso's most famous paintings, the mural Guernica, and a number of other productions from the same period. Biographical accounts are drawn upon to show how aspects of his inner world are revealed in the specific works described and reproduced in this paper. The role of women is shown to have been particularly relevant. The author demonstrates how Picasso's constant pattern of triangular relationships culminated in his personal crisis of 1935, which, together with the Spanish Civil War, reflecting as it did the conflicts of his internal and external relations, contributed to the production of the works in this group. The artist is seen as attempting to work through and make reparation for envious attacks on the parental objects, but it is pointed out that art works should not be assessed by the criterion of therapeutic change.

responseSet.title: ‘From Symbols to Flesh: The Polymorphous Destiny Of Narration’ By Julia Kristeva1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘From Symbols to Flesh

The Polymorphous Destiny Of Narration’ By Julia Kristeva1

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

38 Berkeley Road
London N8 8RU
a.sabbadini@appleonline.net

Andrea Sabbadini  

The paper

Kristeva presents some clinical material about a neurotic woman whose mode of discourse produces a phallic and sadistic domination over her audience/analyst. Kristeva reminds the readers of Freud's remarks on Dora, where he stressed the inability of the hysteric to maintain continuous linear narration, as the tale of hysteria leaves unfilled gaps in the memory, as well as secondarily formed gaps to fill in the original ones. Furthermore, while she agrees that narration is an integral part of free association, Kristeva also contends that free association is not solely about narration, but also comprises other enunciative modalities.

According to the French structuralists, every tale follows a curve of logic that itself forms part of a larger structure where the hero is the grammatical subject, the action is the verb and the dénouement of the plot is the object. The story thus becomes an extension of the logic of the sentence, modulated by the logic of binary conflict (between the hero's good and bad companion) and a quest. As the meaning of the story, or even of a sentence, remains unclear, it requires the participation of the readers to complete the missing meaning with their own creativity.

In psychoanalytic narrative, the object of listening is fantasy, an imaginary act or scenario. What is then, asks Kristeva, the discourse of fantasy? Recent observations, inspired by cognitive psychology, seem to confirm the Kleinian concept of ‘protophantasy’ found in babies: a quasi-narration or ‘pre-narrative envelope’ that articulates drives and desires aimed at the object (the breast) to ensure the survival of the young, phobic and sadistic ego. This pre-narrative envelope requires words from the other to construct itself permanently as phantasy and amounts to an emotional experience based on the drives in an interpersonal context.

Kristeva considers the phallic stage of psychosexual development to be of critical importance in consolidating symbolism. The castration complex steers both the boy and the girl to phallic identification with the father; as we learn from Lacan, the paternal phallus is not only the penis organ, but a representation of the father's symbolic authority over the family triangle. It is indeed through the phallic stage and the consolidation of linguistic symbolisation observable at this stage that the child's narration articulates itself as an interrogation. The curve of the quest, which implies a narration composed of an enigma, an ordeal and a solution, becomes a complex construction which is dependent on the capacity of the subject to overcome the phallic trial to reach the oedipal triangle.

responseSet.title: Kurt R. Eissler (1908-1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kurt R. Eissler (1908-1999)

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York
NY 10028

Aaron Esman  

Kurt R. Eissler, MD, one of the last of the second generation of Viennese psychoanalysts, died in New York on 17 February, 1999 in his 91st year. Predeceased by his wife, Dr Ruth Eissler, he left no survivors.

Born in Vienna on 2 July, 1908, he received the PhD in psychology in 1934 and his MD in 1938, both from the University of Vienna. While working with August Aichhorn in his residential centre for juvenile delinquents, he trained in psychoanalysis at the Vienna Psychoanalytic Institute and was admitted to the Vienna Psychoanalytical Society in 1938. Shortly thereafter, he fled the Nazi occupation and emigrated to the United States, settling first in Chicago, where he achieved certification in psychiatry from the American Board of Psychiatry and Neurology.

Eissler enlisted as a Captain in the US Army Medical Corps in 1943, serving as a military psychiatrist. After the war he relocated to New York, establishing a psychoanalytic practice and joining the New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, where he became a Training and Supervising Analyst and a valued member of the faculty. His first major psychoanalytic publication in the United States was the book he edited in honour of Aichhorn, his former mentor—Searchlights on Delinquency (1949). This was followed by a steady outpouring of papers and books, many of which have become classics in the field. They include his two-volume psychoanalytic biography of Goethe (1963), a major study of Leonardo da Vinci (1961) and a landmark volume on The Psychiatrist and the Dying Patient (1955). His paper ‘The effect of the structure of the ego on psychoanalytic technique’ (1953) established technical principles for a generation of analysts. All of his works were characterised by meticulous scholarship, a spirit of careful and sceptical enquiry and profound psychoanalytic knowledge.

Widely regarded as the conscience of psychoanalysis, Eissler's personal reserve was matched by his dedication to his profession and his untiring efforts to preserve the legacy of Freud and his work. He established the Anna Freud Foundation to provide financial assistance to the Hampstead Clinic in London (now the Anna Freud Centre). One of the founders of the Sigmund Freud Archives, he worked with Anna Freud and others to secure and preserve the crucial documents of psychoanalysis. It was in this setting that he became embroiled in an unfortunate and rancorous dispute with his designated successor as director, one that led to the kind of public controversy he deplored and that was resolved only with some difficulty.

responseSet.title: Corpo-mente e relazione [Body-mind and relationship]: Celestino Genovese. Milan: Dunod/Masson. 1998. Pp. 179.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Corpo-mente e relazione [Body-mind and relationship]

Celestino Genovese. Milan: Dunod/Masson. 1998. Pp. 179.

Teresa Carratelli

Teresa Carratelli

Via Bartolomeo Eustachio 22, 00161 Rome
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, Cantab., MITI)

Teresa Carratelli  

The aim of this collection of essays by Italian psychoanalysts, more than ten years after the death of Eugenio Gaddini, is to promote critical reflection on the ideas of an author who made an original and distinctive contribution to Italian psychoanalysis. Gaddini's thought is of course distinguished by his clinical research and his original contributions on the primitive functioning of the mind, as indicated by the title of the English-language collection of his writings (1992). The book is divided into three sections: (1) ‘Metapsychological considerations’; (2) ‘From the body to the symbol’; and (3) ‘The routes of relationship’.

In the first section, Gaddini's thought is deconstructed and reconstructed with dialectical rigour, although the authors’ profound esteem and affection for their ‘master’ is still evident.

Conrotto and Genovese, in their respective contributions, explore Gaddini's main themes—e.g. the body-mind relationship, the conceptualisation of the self, the origins of creativity (which is seen as the creation of the subject) and the role of the primary relationship in this process of self-creation—and emphasise their ‘creative routes’. These routes also document the process of individuation and differentiation in the construction of Gaddini's scientific thought, in relation to both the doctrinal corpus of Freudian metapsychology and to the influence of Greenacre's theoretical hypotheses on the ontogenetic requirement of the successive phases of development. They also demonstrate his profound knowledge of Winnicott's ideas. Gaddini got to know Winnicott through the dialogue entertained by his wife Renata with the British psychoanalyst. This was unprecedented for Italy.

Conrotto and Genovese have much to say about narcissism concerning the relationship between Gaddini's and Winnicott's thought. They discuss the criticism (for example, of Grinberg and Mitchell) that these authors distort Freud's concept of narcissism.

According to Conrotto, Gaddini performed ‘a repression of narcissism’ by speculating on the pre-drive, or non-drive, levels of psychic functioning and distinguishing the psychosensory area, fuelled by a non-drive, environment-imitating energy, from the drive-based psycho-oral area. The writer draws attention to the relevant note by the book's editor, Genovese, who does not subscribe to Winnicott's and Gaddini's thesis concerning the repression of narcissism. He holds that the Freudian hypothesis of primary narcissism is neither rejected by Winnicott nor inconsistent with his hypothesis of ‘the subjective use of the object’—i.e. the early use made of the primary relationship by the object. It is also pointed out that the so-called primitive functioning of the self, involving imitation through perception in Gaddini's well known conceptualisation, both concerns and fertilises the area of primary narcissism. This is a dimension of self-experience that the individual seeks out because it constitutes a fundamental guarantee of the reality of self, facilitated by attachment to the subjective object. In the absence of the real object, therefore, the psychic proto-model of imitation, imitation through being, has the aim of omnipotently re-establishing the fusion of self and object and of guaranteeing a minimum sense of consistency (cum-exsistere).

<autaff affid="af001"><addr><ln>São Paulo</ln></addr></autaff><autaff affid="af002"><addr><ln>Boston, MA</ln><ln>75 Intervale Road</ln><ln>Newton</ln><ln>MA 02459</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Mother and Baby
responseSet.authorMast: Marisa Pelella Melega and  James Herzog
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mother and Baby

Marisa Melega and James Herzog

James Herzog

São Paulo

Boston, MA
75 Intervale Road
Newton
MA 02459

James Herzog  

The panel was opened by Marisa Pelella Melega, who established a historical perspective and introduced the panellists. The first presentation was made by Joy Osofsky, from New Orleans. Her paper was entitled: ‘The parent-infant relationship and infant mental health’. Osofsky reviewed the importance of reciprocity in the parent infant relationship, citing recent work on intergenerational transmission of attachment capabilities and liabilities. This led her to a discussion of risk factors in the earliest relationship and the particularly jeopardising potential of a maternal traumatic history. Osofsky highlighted the skewing of the early mother child relationship that can result from adolescent parenthood and from maternal depression.

The presentation briefly touched on the issue of relational representation and the staying power of initial representations as well as their plasticity, and then proceeded to a discussion of trauma as a pathogenic factor in young children and models of treatment that might benefit both relational aberrations and resultant traumatic disruptions. Osofsky championed approaches that utilise successive approximations of preceding traumas in a fashion that both detoxifies and fosters increasing mastery and competence.

Esperanza Perez de Pla, from Mexico, presented a hospital-based intervention with a child with multiple functional and psychological dilemmas. Her paper, entitled: ‘Affects, body and word in the mother-baby relationship; reflections derived from clinical material’, tells the story of Paloma, a ‘failure-to-thrive’ child with oesophageal reflux and respiratory difficulties and her mother, Ana. With skill and sensitivity, the team diagnosed the relational, transgenerational and individual aspects of Paloma' painful intrapsychic and interactive situation. Focusing on her crying and psychomotricity as affective manifestations of underlying representational and drive-infused conflicts, Perez de Pla detailed the ways in which symptoms were understood and what interventions flowed from these understandings. She concluded by saying: ‘We could say that the unconscious is structured between two bodies (mother and child). The libidinal history is being engraved in that initial corporeal ego always in relation with the primordial other, the mother with her history and own desires. In this process, everything seems to demonstrate that affect takes a determinant function at the level of sense’. Perez de Pla acknowledged the influence of André Green' On Private Madness (1987, New York: Int. Univ. Press) in her formulation of this schema.

responseSet.title: Judith Kestenberg (1910–1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Judith Kestenberg (1910–1999)

Ira Brenner

Ira Brenner

10 Presidential Blvd
Bela Cynwyd
PA 19004

Ira Brenner  

The last twenty years of Judith Kestenberg's life coincided with my first twenty years as a psychoanalyst. During that time she became one of the most influential people in my life. Although she was 68 when I first met her, her energy, enthusiasm and vigour were so astounding that I quickly realised that I would have to run in order to keep up with her. Despite her diminutive stature (she was 4' 11”) she had a huge presence and a gift for inspiring colleagues and students throughout the world. She had an unwavering quest for intellectual truth and understanding, combined with a warmth, a tenderness and a playful twinkle in her eye whenever she delighted in the antics of a young child.

Her illustrious career as a child analyst who pioneered research in development and in the effects of psychic trauma began like many individuals of her generation, in Europe. Born in Cracow, Poland, and raised in Tarnow, she once described what influenced the direction of her life's work:

When Europe began to recuperate from the ravages of World War 1, a great number of children were orphaned and homeless. In my small home town … was an institution in which children of all ages grew up under the watchful eye of an authoritarian director. It was my mother who helped me realize that the infants and toddlers among them desperately needed a home like atmosphere. We both persuaded my generous father to create a small home for ten young children. My mother took me there often; while she discussed matters of policy with the nurses, I played with the babies (1975, p. xv).

This was from the preface of her now classic 1975 book, Children and Parents, p. xv. And Judy played with the babies for the rest of her life, integrating her extraordinary powers of observation with her formidable intellect and making enduring contributions spanning seven decades. When her declining health made it impossible for her to deny that she could not work any longer (for example we completed our book while she was still on a respirator recuperating from open-heart surgery), she reluctantly retired. The most important appointments she held at that time were Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at NYU, Training Analyst Emeritus for adults and children in the Division of Psychoanalytic Education at NYU, and Pediatric Research Psychiatrist at Long Island Jewish Hillside Medical Center. She was the founder and director of Child Development Research in Long Island, a foundation under the guidance of the International Study of Organized Persecution of Children that sponsored her landmark research on child survivors of the Holocaust which has collected over 1,500 interviews worldwide. It also ran the Center for Parents and Children which for over twenty years studied early development and primary prevention.

responseSet.title: The Couch and the Tree: Dialogues in Psychoanalysis and Buddhism: Anthony Molino. London: Constable. 1999. Pp. 361.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Couch and the Tree: Dialogues in Psychoanalysis and Buddhism

Anthony Molino. London: Constable. 1999. Pp. 361.

David Black

David Black

30 Cholmley Gardens, London NW6 1AG

David Black  

This book is that thing which James Strachey said did not exist: a cake made of nothing but currants. It is an anthology of writings on Buddhism and psychoanalysis, mostly by psychoanalysts of different schools, but sometimes by Buddhist teachers. There are thirty pieces in all, including three interviews, between Jung and the Zen teacher Hisamatsu, between Joyce McDougall and the Dalai Lama and between Anthony Molino and Nina Coltart. If I say it is composed entirely of currants, I mean to imply that the shrewd reader will enjoy it, but will probably choose to consume it more slowly than the driven reviewer.

Molino has put together a fascinating variety of texts. He begins with a historical section, stretching back to the 1920s and the exuberant writing of Joe Tom Sun (also known as the Chicago psychoanalyst, Joseph Thompson), and a splendid period-piece by Franz Alexander, ‘Buddhistic training as an artificial catatonia’. In Civilization and its Discontents, Freud had suggested that one function of religions was to promote ‘the restoration of limitless narcissism’ (1930, S.E. 21, p. 72). Alexander goes beyond Freud, to see Buddhist practice as the systematic pursuit of psychotic states of mind. The early levels of meditation are an ‘experimentally induced melancholia’, with all libido converted into sadistic frenzy against the self; catatonia soon follows, and the state of Enlightenment, in which the Buddha remembers his previous lives in many different life-forms, is the ‘unrolling’ of embryological development, ‘which is nothing other than a short repetition of all forms of life in the geological rise and fall of many worlds since the first birth’ (p. 20).

Alexander's text reads as if by someone who has never met a practising Buddhist.

833

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le Séminaire. Livre V. Les formations de l'inconscient [The Seminar. Book V. The formations of the unconscious] (1957-1958)

Jacques Lacan. Paris: Seuil. 1998. Pp. 518.

Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

48 boulevard Beaumarchais, 75011 Paris
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Gilbert Diatkine  

Despite Lacan's considerable influence in Europe and America, most of his teaching material still remains unpublished. Of the promised twenty-six volumes of his Séminaire, only ten have appeared so far. Book V, published in Paris in 1998, covers the academic year 1957-1958. It should be read as the record of a body of ideas in the throes of development, replete with contradictions and repetitions in its attempt to convince an audience made up not only of analysts in training but also of colleagues, patients and many cultivated individuals interested in psychoanalysis. A seminar of texts whose watchword is ‘back to Freud’, it includes a detailed study of Jokes and Their Relation to the Unconscious (1905b), as well as more superficial treatments of several other writings (“‘A child is being beaten”’ [1919], the Dora case [1905a] and the case of Elizabeth von R [Freud & Breuer, 1893-95], Totem and Taboo [1912-13], and several dreams from The Interpretation of Dreams [1900]). In his enthusiasm, Lacan often quite seriously attributes his own ideas to Freud—for example, the ‘desire for recognition’ (p. 245), the ‘phallus signifier’ (p. 378), the ‘signifier’ (for instance, p. 233 and p. 235), and imaginary identifications (p. 227). However, despite this return to the inventor of psychoanalysis, he does not conceal his criticisms. He considers that Freud's chapter on the comic is worthless (p. 109). The Nirvana principle and the death drive make him ‘faintly … smile’ (p. 246). The idea that ‘ontogenesis reproduces phylogenesis’ seems to him a ‘fundamental reference of modern obscurantism [which is] more idiotic than the … catechism’ (p. 315). Freud's interpretive technique is stated to be wrong: Freud gives advice and his interventions are off the point (p. 322). Lacan does not accept the economic point of view, for example in the understanding of jokes. In particular, like other analysts at this time, he deprecates the fact that Freud's topographies have no place for the object (p. 424).

responseSet.title: Shared Life Narratives in the Work of Lou Andreas-Salomé
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shared Life Narratives in the Work of Lou Andreas-Salomé

Jaap Bos

Jaap Bos

PO Box 80140
3508 TC, Utrecht
The Netherlands

Jaap Bos  

The author presents a re-reading of the works of Lou Andreas-Salomé (1861-1937), one of the key figures in the early history of psychoanalysis. He focusses in particular on her biographical and autobiographical works, as well as her correspondences with Freud, Rilke and other influential people of her time, arguing that Andreas-Salomé disappears from her own works as an autonomous speaking subject, while reappearing in the works of others as a silent, tacit influence. On closer examination, a specific genre emerges from her works that has so far gone unnoticed: the shared life narrative. The author claims that Andreas-Salomé developed this genre of shared life narratives in an attempt to match her theoretical notions of narcissism to a practical communicative position that is neither subjectivistic nor objectivistic. Relating the notion of shared life narratives to the psychoanalytic discourse, new possibilities may be opened up for expanding and enlarging our knowledge of human interaction.

responseSet.title: Building Bridges: The Negotiation of Paradox in Psychoanalysis: Stuart A. Pizer. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1998. Pp. 220.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Building Bridges: The Negotiation of Paradox in Psychoanalysis

Stuart A. Pizer. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1998. Pp. 220.

Barbara Stimmel

Barbara Stimmel

1185 Park Avenue, New York, NY 10128

Barbara Stimmel  

This book is illustrative of an element in the swirling controversy in psychoanalysis as practised and conceived in the United States. In this review, I hope to bring to the international readership a flavour of this state of affairs in a country which prides itself on its diversity, but which also struggles constantly with the resulting strains that are felt in our profession.

In a book ambitious in scope, Stuart Pizer is attempting to bring to life, with evocative clinical examples and extensive descriptive language, the kind of work done by a relational psychoanalyst; for example, Part II builds from genders to species to nations. Pizer's clinical writing gives a full flavour of the way he and the relational group work. It is far too easy and even tempting for the reviewer to respond to others’ clinical descriptions from her own perspective. A greater problem than simply opposing one unprovable clinical point with another is that we have not yet discovered an effective way to cross the clinical chasm that separates several of our schools of thought. I would prefer to point out that this book should help the reader to define more clearly where she stands in relation to a segment of the analytic population which is developing an increasingly strong voice.

Pizer uses his book as a platform to elaborate on the concept of intervention found in a quote by Loewald. Pizer interprets this idea, concerning the mobilisation of patients’ ego function, as the conversion of intervention into negotiation. Negotiation, with its references to business, mutual agreement, transfer of authority and ownership, management and coping, is a term that furthers the agenda of relational psychoanalysis, particularly its emphasis on conscious interactions. Without knowing how Loewald would feel about this appropriation of his ideas, it is important to remember that when building on forebears, (a constant activity in all intellectual endeavours in which we all engage), certain sacred cows are invoked which invest dialogue and disagreement with other meanings. For example, relational psychoanalysis claims Winnicott as one of its heroes as an analyst who was in touch with the child in the adult, and willing to cross the transitional divide separating patient and analyst. Pizer relies heavily on Winnicott and his squiggle game, as one example, averring that we can play with our patients and in that way share our subjectivity. Confronting some of the problems in Winnicott's theory-making is rarely done ironically, even as with problems in Freud's theory-making that are fair game, as they should be.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical And Theoretical Uses Of Bi-Logic

Presentations In Honour Of Matte Blanco

José Infante and Warren Procci

Warren Procci

SANTIAGO
230 S. San Rafael Avenue
Pasadena
CA 91105-1525

Warren Procci  

It was fitting that an IPA Congress in Chile should devote a major panel to the contributions of the Chilean analyst, Matte Blanco. There was a relatively large audience, which included many Chileans, as well as a broad overall representation, and a panel that included his widow, as well as scholars and colleagues intimately familiar with his work. For easy, smoother readability, I will present the contributions of the panellists grouped by subject matter rather than in the order of their presentation.

Gerald Wooster of London provided a ‘primer’ on Matte Blanco's work, which established a framework for the panel. Matte Blanco's best-known contribution is his concept of bi-logic. He stated that there are two kinds of logic operating in the psychological world: asymmetrical logic and symmetrical logic. The first kind, asymmetrical logic, is characterised as that used by ‘hard’ scientists and by psychologists. It is the logic upon which mathematics and science are built. It is the logic that enables us to discriminate between all the categories of difference in the world, such as differences in time, space, whole and part, syntax, grammar, language, gender, or to discriminate within any kind of classification; i.e. with this form of logic we discern differences, the lack of symmetry. It is the logic that has priority in consciousness and appears to have links in brain function with what we call dominant hemisphere functions. It is the outstanding contributor to our cognitive function.

The second kind of logic is symmetrical logic. By contrast with asymmetrical logic, symmetrical logic is characterised by examining for similarities and analogies in all that we perceive and think about. Some evidence suggests that the functioning of this part of our mind corresponds to subdominant hemisphere brain functioning and that it helps creative functioning. The logic is called symmetrical because of a simple test that can be applied to any proposition that comes to it for judgement. If we take a statement and if we consider its converse, both statements convey the same meaning in symmetrical logic, whereas in asymmetrical logic turning the statement round the other way renders it nonsensical.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sindromi psicosociali. La psicoanalisi e le patologie sociali [Psychosocial syndromes. Psychoanalysis and social pathologies]

Giuseppe Di Chiara. Milan: Raffaello Cortina. 1999. Pp. 109.

Giangaetano Bartolomei and Sandra Filippini

Sandra Filippini

c/o Via del Moro, 9, 50123 Florence
(Translated by Philip Slotkin MA Cantab., MITI)

Sandra Filippini  

Giuseppe Di Chiara has for some time taken a particular interest in the psychoanalytic exploration of social phenomena, His previous writings on nature and culture have concerned destructiveness as a psychosocial syndrome, tolerance in relation to processes of integration and disintegration, psychoanalysis and politics, groups, and so on. With this slender and concise volume, he now returns to the subject of psycho-social syndromes, this time using psychoanalysis as an instrument for understanding some of the fundamental mechanisms that underlie social pathologies.

‘Psychosocial syndromes’ are social situations that can serve as pathological defences. They constitute forms of collective behaviour that cause predictable immediate or future problems. However, such behaviour persists despite the predictability of its adverse effects. While Di Chiara argues that its motivating forces can, in principle, be reduced in strength, he believes that such behaviour is an expression of powerful anxieties of unconscious origin that are shared by society (p. 3f.).

In the volume's twelve short chapters, the author identifies and briefly defines some of the principal psychosocial syndromes. A good example is narcissism, understood in the same sense as in Lasch's well-known The Culture of Narcissism, which Di Chiara quotes—that is, as a social group defence ‘in the direction of selfishness, hedonistic satisfaction … and competitiveness’ (p. 12). Other instances are some of the specific forms assumed by class-consciousness, and the exercise of power as an end in itself, distinct from the function of ‘government’, with which it is contrasted. ‘Government is exercised in the context of a working group’, Di Chiara writes, ‘and power in that of a “basic-assumption” group’ (p. 55). The mechanisms of legitimisation of the leader therefore differ greatly in the two cases. The charismatic leader of a totalitarian regime is held to be ‘the Lord's anointed’: ‘Hitler was deemed Führer not because of his appointment as Reich Chancellor but on account of his personal qualities, which were regarded as exceptional. Conversely, a pope or a king owes his legitimacy to his office’ (p. 54).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'indifferenza dell'anima [The indifference of the soul]

Lucio Russo. Rome: Borla. 1998. Pp. 267.

Paola Marion

Paola Marion

Via Pasubio 4, 00195 Rome,
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Paola Marion  

As the author immediately makes clear in his introduction, this intriguingly and evocatively titled book is concerned with the exploration and investigation of the ‘undifferentiated’levels of the psyche—i.e. emotional states of oceanic void, or confused areas of the mind. The volume is divided into eight chapters, some of which comprise papers previously presented at scientific events and published elsewhere, and its very structure is tantamount to a declaration that Russo's research is the cumulative result of a long personal career—as is borne out by the changes than have taken place over time. The author emphasises that his reflections are based on a body of clinical experience extending over many years; and he also pays tribute to the group he formed, which has shared his experience of research on melancholia.

Russo belongs to a tightly knit circle of Italian psychoanalysts with a common interest in what they call the analytic relation and a specific style, which is evident both in their theoretical references and in their particular reading of the Freudian corpus and of post-Freudian psychoanalysis. The wealth of quotations from his oeuvre confirms that Freud is the author's main interlocutor. Post-Freudian psychoanalysis is allotted a limited space, within which the predominant references are to French-language psychoanalysis and some of the authors who have specifically studied twilight states of the mind (Grunberger, Jacobson, Rosolato and Abraham & Torok).

1238

responseSet.title: ‘M’ (1931)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘M’ (1931)11

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel and Fritz Lang

Fritz Lang

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel
82 rue de l'Université
75007 Paris

Distributor: UFA

Fritz Lang  

The premonitory character of the film M [Mörder unter uns] and its connection with the rise of Nazism has been indicated more than once. I would like to demonstrate the essential ambiguity in this linkage.

It is said that a masterpiece allows interpretations at multiple levels. According to Lang himself (though should we ever believe auteurs?), he just wanted to make a ‘documentary’ about a criminal and the process of police investigation. He tells us that he was inspired by The Threepenny Opera and by a news report about Berlin's organised crime mob searching for an unknown killer to get rid of the insistent presence of the police. To Lang then, the film is the hunt for a criminal and his subsequent judgement by the mob.

The Nazis themselves, already powerful enough in 1931 to dictate the law, forced Lang to change the original title of the film Mörder unter uns (Murderer[s] among us) to M, probably because they felt he was alluding to them! Rarely is a translated title better than the original, but the alliteration in M le Maudit (M the cursed)2 makes this more stirring than the original German title.

Although there had been the case of Peter Kürten, ‘The Vampire of Düsseldorf’, Lang and his wife, Thea von Harbou, co-authors of the screenplay, were inspired by the Grossman case in Berlin, as well as by other killers of children such as ‘The Ogre of Hanover’.

The recurrence of this type of serial murder might be understood as a social symptom. Certainly, it is only in retrospect that these crimes, which appear to be isolated cases, make sense. In France the Weidman case is often brought to mind. Weidman killed at least seven people and was arrested on 9 December, 1938. He was born in Frankfurt and executed in Versailles on 17 June, 1939. This was the last public execution in France and it took place in the midst of unexpected agitation in the crowd, as if it were a premonitory event, heralding war, crime and the lawlessness of Nazism that would expand throughout Europe. This is a topic that Michel Tournier revisits in his 1970 novel Le Roi des Aulnes [The Erl-King].

[[RunningHead]]

Response

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

Dear Sir,

I very much appreciate Dr Goldberg's continued interest in the editorial on memory and his clarification of our points of disagreement.

It seems to me axiomatic that memory is an interactive set of systems. My memory of myself (encoded episodically into the explicit, declarative system of autobiographical memory) will inevitably include memories of how I acted in a particular situation (behaviour which was strongly influenced by my procedural memory system), and therefore the interaction of memory systems as part of normal everyday function cannot be doubted. I am, however, clearer now why Dr Goldberg questioned this. I think he was concerned with interaction at the level of units or modules in the brain. However, whether these two memory systems do or do not interact at the neural level isn't the point. The two systems must inevitably interact when the mind is conceived of as a functional unit and not as separate processing systems. The issue is not whether one memory system influences the other at the neural level, but rather if one system provides material for the other via behaviour. Of course, there may also be interactions between the two at the level of brain function but that is a more complex question and not at issue here.

Dr Goldberg is absolutely correct that similar ideas concerning the role of memory in therapeutic change have been advanced previously. As Paolo Migone also pointed out in his letter to the Editor (2000), Franz Alexander was eloquent on this issue. Do these ideas need pointing out again? Judging purely from the response an earlier version of my paper received when I presented it at a special meeting on recovered memories at the British Psycho-Analytical Society, I think they do. Several senior colleagues expressed strong disagreement with my position, claiming great importance for the recovery of episodic memories in analysis. In the editorial I tried to express my belief that these memories emerge as a consequence of the activation of past or new relationship representations and that the change in relationship representations is the curative aspect of the process, not the recovery of memories (true or false). Dr Goldberg asks: ‘Are there really analysts who believe otherwise?’. Perhaps he is correct in assuming that there are few; however, Victoria Hamilton's (1996) excellent book on psychoanalysts' implicit theories of change would suggest otherwise. So would Sandler & Dreher's (1997)

5 bis, rue Le Tasse, 75116 Paris

This book by Michèle Perron-Borelli, who is well known in France for her publications on the ‘dynamics’ of fantasy, is a robust theoretical construction that rests on the firmest of Freudian foundations. Its author here undertakes a comprehensive exploration of the status and organising role of fantasy activity in the formation of the psyche, and in so doing puts forward a number of new hypotheses.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

Dear Sir,

I very much appreciated Dr Holmes's intelligent commentary on the Guest Editorial and I am very pleased that it appeared to be broadly consistent with the point of view he has espoused for some years and for which I have a deep respect.

He extends the argument in three ways. First, building on the synergies between declarative and implicit memory systems, he emphasises how the therapeutic listener preconsciously

354

evaluates explicit recollections in the light of information implicitly supplied through the transference. This is undoubtedly what happens and the claims made for therapeutic intuitions about the veracity of recovered memories (e.g. Person & Klar, 1994) must be founded on this kind of process.

His second point is consistent with that of Migone (Migone & Liotti, 1998), and by implication that of Franz Alexander. The re-emergence of memories is not intrinsically linked to the undoing of repression, but can occur because of a reorganisation of the representation of self-object relationships. While Migone's emphasis is on changes brought about by alterations in emotional relationship configurations, Holmes's emphasis is on self-in-relation-to-other representational structures. I believe both points of view refer to the same process, albeit at different levels of analysis. I think both these contributors would agree that affective experience arises as a consequence of the activation of specific relationship schemas. Perhaps the best current psychoanalytic model to integrate these points of view is that of Otto Kernberg (1988). He postulates the existence of basic mental units that combine relationship representation with predominant affective states (self-other-affect triads).

Dr Holmes and I have a slight disagreement concerning his third point, although this is more likely to be a matter of emphasis than a difference in principle. He suggests that successful therapy is marked by openness and honesty with self and others, which is based on an improved capacity to identify distortion. A re-evaluation of memories may be a part of this change and will, in the case of favourable therapeutic outcome, invariably occur in the direction of tempering the patient's omnipotence. However, I do not see that it follows from this that the movement will be necessarily towards the more ‘genuine’, unless this word is used in its broadest possible sense, which also encompasses that which is subjectively true. I do not believe that memories of a well-functioning individual are any more likely to be ‘genuine’, in the sense of accurate, than that of a poorly functioning individual. In fact we have some preliminary data to support the opposite view. In the follow-up of individuals assessed and treated in childhood at the Anna Freud Centre, in general we found more idealised memories of childhood among well-functioning adults than we did among those who continued to experience problems as many as two or three decades after their original assessment. It may well be that these memories were felt subjectively to be more genuine to the well-functioning individuals, even though they were more artefactual compared to the historical record. However, this latter argument is evidently circular. The genuineness of experience, a sense of coherence concerning one's past, may simply be a hallmark of healthy functioning.

responseSet.title: The Real Thing? Some Thoughts On Boys Don't Cry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Real Thing? Some Thoughts On Boys Don't Cry

Donald Moss and Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place
New York
NY 10003

Lynne Zeavin  

Director: Kimberley Pierce

Distributor: Fox Searchlight

Boys Don't Cry, written and directed by Kimberley Pierce, is a film provoked by the 1993 rape and murder of Teena Brandon/Brandon Teena, a 21-year-old transsexual. Brandon was killed in rural Nebraska, to which he had fled from Lincoln, where, Pierce sketchily, and light-heartedly, informs us, life had been laced with the intermittent pleasures and steady difficulties of trying to live as though a boy. Once relocated, Brandon won the friendship of a number of the town's young men, and the love of one of its most desired young women. When Brandon's neurotic penchant for acting out leads to yet another brief stay in a local jail, personal history and biological gender combine to expose her. This exposure eventually leads to her rape and murder.

Pierce, a New Yorker, dropped everything to attend the trial and, for months afterwards, lived amongst people who had constituted Brandon's last circle of intimates. The film, then, is a kind of case report. But rather than primarily study Brandon's transsexuality, the film presents her transsexual inclinations as a series of euphoric conquests. The film focuses on a range of anxious reactions to her transsexuality. Its strategy is comparable, perhaps, to using the particulars of the Dora case not for what they might reveal about female hysteria, but for what they might reveal about misogyny. The internal anguish wrought by and determining Brandon's sexual confusion is mostly left for the viewer to imagine and fill in. This gap will be particularly noticeable to a clinically inclined viewer. As viewers, we are given only intermittent glimpses into the costs of Brandon's daily sexual transgressions. These glimpses are, in part, meant to remind us of the costs of our own daily economies of transgression and compliance.

In her film, Pierce inserts the unconventional problems of transsexuality into a conventional narrative structure. Throughout the film Brandon is presented as a doomed, though beguiling and beautiful rascal, recognisably located in the lineage of well-known cinematic bad-boys like James Dean, Steve McQueen and Paul Newman. Like these predecessors, Brandon's heroic stature derives from her unwillingness to compromise her identity. Unlike them, though, the identity in question is in an unremitting and overt ‘sexual crisis’. Pierce presents Brandon's struggles against biological determinism as the struggles of a dignified renegade.

responseSet.title: Memory and therapeutic action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memory and therapeutic action

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg  

Dear Sir,

It is good of Dr Fonagy to respond to my letter in the spirit of debating a controversy, and I hope he and others continue to see it in that sense.

Fonagy begins by wondering why I claim procedural memory to play no part in autobiographical memory. I gave the reference to that in my earlier letter and I explained that autobiographical memory is composed of episodic memory, which is defined as information remembered within a particular temporal or spatial context (Tulving, 1983). Indeed, procedural memory is quite different from both episodic and semantic memory (Hendel et al., 1993). Fonagy supports his position (1999b) by reference to Kandel, but that author's paper has to do with declarative and procedural memory and, of course, is not responsive to what I claimed is relevant to autobiographical memory (Nelson, 1996). But it is perhaps most important to recognise that Kandel's position, which Fonagy claims to represent, is really of no support whatsoever to what Fonagy appeared to say in his editorial (1999a) about the importance of the recognition and independence of procedural memory. Indeed others, such as Muller & Overton say: ‘in fact, the notions of procedural and declarative knowledge do not appear to be very useful for differentiating between knowledge levels’ (1998, p. 79) and ‘Recent developments in attentional theory cast doubt on the possibility of any strict distinction between procedural and declarative knowledge’ (p. 78).

Likewise the attribution of one's acquisition of moral rules to this category of memory is best credited to Immanuel Kant (1787 [1961]) rather than presented as a new finding. Once Fonagy agrees in his reply that these categories of memory have ‘nothing to do with psychoanalytic treatment’, one can move beyond the question of just why they were brought up in the first place to what he claims as the essential point or points of his editorial. The first, that the recovery of memories is not curative was long ago clearly laid out by Franz Alexander, who said: ‘Remembering, accordingly, is more an indicator of a successful removal of a repression than anything else. It is more the result or a sign of the progress of the analysis than the cause of it’ (1940, p. 146). On page 218 of his editorial Fonagy says just about the same thing as if it were an idea of his own. Are there really analysts who believe otherwise?

responseSet.title: Blood and Love
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blood and Love

James Herzog

James Herzog

75 Intervale Road
Newton Center
MA 02459

James Herzog  

In this clinical presentation both transgenerationally transmitted and directly inflicted trauma are examined as constituents in the psychic life of a gifted little girl called Natalia. Her attempts to eroticise and thus modulate those aspects of her experience which have deformed her play processes are described and the ways in which the analysis served to help her both recognise and regulate this material is detailed. The paper is organised around material from the opening phase of the work, the play which characterised the middle of the analysis and the examination of an essay which the patient wrote in the closing phase. The central role of affect for each participant in the analytic work is stressed as a pivotal component of the play process.

responseSet.title: The Recovered Memories Controversy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Recovered Memories Controversy

Phil Mollon

Phil Mollon

Phil Mollon  

Dear Sir,

Attempts to throw light on the murky waters of the recovered memory debate so often end up obscuring more than they reveal. Moreover, theorising designed to insulate psychoanalysis from the controversy may do so at the expense of foreclosure of important elements—and may indeed offer a false solution.

I refer to Fonagy's guest editorial on ‘Memory and the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis’ (IJPA, 80:215-23) and Brenneis's review of my book Remembering Trauma. A Psychotherapist's Guide to Memory and Illusion (IJPA, 80:614-16).

Although helpful and clarifying in many ways, Fonagy's sophisticated theorising may actually function to eclipse certain issues in relation to psychoanalysis and memory. For example, he appears to confuse recovery of memory with the reconstruction of childhood development on the basis of transference—as in the following remarks:

‘Notwithstanding Kris's (1956) revolutionary reformulation of the psychoanalytic understanding of memory, the majority of ego-psychologists have continued to place emphasis on the therapeutic efficacy of reconstruction …’ (p. 215) and ‘it is hard to envision the formulation of an accurate reconstruction on the basis of a patient's distorted memories and symptoms’ (p. 216).

By implication, therefore, he suggests that those psychoanalysts who place emphasis on reconstruction of childhood are ‘recovered memory therapists’. But reconstruction is not about recovery of memory. It is concerned with building a picture of the patient's childhood development. Freud's classic example of reconstruction of the Wolf Man's childhood was an instance not of a patient's recovered memory but the analyst's reconstruction of a complex infantile neurosis. The ‘psychical object’, which Freud, in his 1937 paper on ‘Constructions in analysis’, considers to be potentially available for reconstruction because ‘all the essentials are preserved’ (p. 260) is not a memory but an earlier mental organisation—this is surely the meaning of the ‘archaeological metaphor’.

responseSet.title: Affect Regulation
responseSet.authorMast: Mortimer Ostow and  Gail C. Bates
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affect Regulation

Mortimer Ostow and Gail Bates

Gail Bates

New York

881 Fremont Avenue, B7
Los Altos
CA 94024

Gail Bates  

Recent developments in neuroscience and psychopharmacology, and the widening range of patients suffering from trauma seen in psychoanalytical treatment, shed new light on the problems of affect regulation.

The Moderator, Mortimer Ostow, asserted that recent emphases in psychoanalysis on the ideational aspects of affect had neglected an understanding and clarification of the more biological aspect of character that is ‘mood’. In his view, mood generates ideation and can be thought of as an instinctual reaction to a biological state. Advances in neuroscience related to emotional states have brought evidence regarding the alterations of brain chemistry and strengthened the view that there may be more inherent biological factors that determine mood than was previously thought. Ostow strongly emphasised the value of pharmacology to stabilise mood. He noted that since 1962 he had actively proposed the use of psychopharmacological agents when indicated as an adjunct to psychoanalysis.

The first panellist, Henry Krystal, gave a paper entitled ‘Optimizing affect function for psychoanalysis’. His main focus was on patients who suffer from alexithymia. He observed that these patients lack an ability to identify what they experience emotionally, are stiff and constricted. They use action, psychosomatic mechanisms or substances to manage affect and they experience emotion as frightening or frustrating. The alexithymia patient is difficult to treat psychoanalytically and may require initial modifications of technique as well as medication.

Krystal elaborated two pathways to alexithymia. First, infantile psychic trauma rooted in the failure of emotional containment in the mother/infant dayad. The repetitious lack of containment may leave an infant in an overwhelmed state of mind resulting in inconsolability. Subsequently, there is an inability to develop adequate means of tolerating frustration, managing feelings and the phantasies associated with them that interferes with the process of symbolisation. The patient' hatred and fear of emotional life is a defence against the experience of overwhelming psychic catastrophe. The inability to know and identify emotional states, to tolerate them and to use affect as internal communication is characteristic of these patients.

responseSet.title: Affects in Men and Women: Gender Differences
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affects in Men and Women

Gender Differences

Susana Dupetit and Frances Thomson-Salo

Frances Thomson-Salo

Buenos Aires

PO Box 2226
Caulfield Junction
Melbourne
VIC 3161

Frances Thomson-Salo  

This was a challenging panel for which to be a Reporter, as the Moderator and two panellists decided that their presentations would be read not in English but in Spanish and French, without simultaneous translation being available, and so the discussion cannot be fully reported.

Overall there was quite a marked divide between the point of view as represented on the one hand by the Moderator, Susana Dupetit, and presenters Mathilde Caplansky and Evelyne Séchaud, and on the other hand that expressed by Beth Seelig. Caplansky and particularly Séchaud recapitulated the theory of sexuality outlined by Freud, and Dupetit in introducing the papers said that all three presenters emphasised castration as the loss of love. Seelig pointed out that this was not part of her paper. Her presentation spanned several disciplines in an attempt to integrate what they have to offer to psychoanalytic theorising.

Caplansky's hypothesis, illustrated by two short clinical vignettes and two poems, was that men and women experience the same affects but there are gender-related differences in the way they express them. Primarily this consists of what she calls dissociation in the case of men, and the ‘eternal demand for company’ in the case of women. Caplansky saw these differences as stemming from the earliest experiences in mother-infant interaction. She conceptualised affect as the result of a ‘sensorial drive complex’ that comes into play in the field of inter-subjectivity. In the very early mother-infant relationship boys and girls are equally ‘at the mercy’ of the maternal function or dysfunction, and the experiences the child has at this time contain the key to the psychic differences in the expression of affects. As man is brought into the world by woman and raised by her he is involved for the rest of his life in the task of convincing himself and others that he has escaped from that—that he is not a woman nor a baby, nor a homosexual. To get away from that experience of absolute dependency he uses the ‘dramatic affective dissociation’ common to men in western culture: that is, to be tender and express affects openly has a connotation of weakness and vulnerability, traits which are seen as belonging to the female gender.

Corpo—mente: studi clinici sulla patologia psicosomatica in eta evolutiva. [Body—mind: clinical studies on psychosomatic pathology in young children]

Marta Badoni

Marta Badoni

Via Cosimo Del Fante 3, 20122 Milan
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Marta Badoni  

This book's two editors are Teresa Carratelli, psychoanalyst and associate professor, and Anna Maria Lanza, a psychotherapist engaged in child psychiatry research, both at Rome University. The appearance of this eagerly awaited volume is fitting for a number of reasons: first, as a tribute to the work of a school, established in the early 1980s and centred on the second Chair of Child Neuropsychiatry at Rome's ‘La Sapienza’ University, which studies and treats psychosomatic pathology in young children;7 second, as homage to two masters who unfortunately both died prematurely, Eugenio Gaddini, former President of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society (SPI), and Adriano Giannotti, training analyst at the SPI and incumbent of the Chair just mentioned; and, finally, because the editors seem to have caught the right moment to publish the fruits of the authors’ labours and make them accessible to a wider academic readership. After all, the path from the impossibility of owning psychic pain to the development of somatic symptoms appears to be particularly well—trodden today, and a book which, by virtue of its structure, constitutes a sagacious guide to their exploration is therefore welcome.

The introduction by Mario Bertolini and the foreword by Andreas Giannakoulas, both of whom are training analysts at the Italian Psychoanalytical Association (AiPsi) and have been engaged for many years in research and training in child and adolescent psychoanalysis, confirm the aptness of the volume's publication at this juncture.

The book is divided into two parts of approximately equal length, devoted to theory and clinical practice respectively. The background is the thought of Winnicott and his conception of the psyche—soma.

It should be noted first of all that, because psychosomatic disorders in children can be studied by working with the child within and together with the environment in which he is growing up and perhaps expressing its malaise, there are better opportunities than with adults suffering from psychosomatic symptoms to compare the internal worlds of the child and his parents and to observe the intense interplay of projections and the role of unconscious fantasies.

responseSet.title: Egoyan's Exotica: Where does The Real Horror Reside?1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Egoyan's Exotica

Where does The Real Horror Reside?1

Ronit Matalon and Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905
Israel
Emanuel@psy.haifa.ac.il

Emanuel Berman  

Director: Atom Egoyan

Distributor: Buena Vista Home Video

To me, the highest aim of any film is to enter so completely into the subconscious of the viewer that there are moments and scenes and gestures which can be generated by the spectator's imagination. That becomes part of the film they're playing in their mind, and I hope the film has enough space to allow that type of room, that type of exchange (Egoyan, 1995, p. 50).

‘What have we really seen?’

This question unites Exotica's protagonists and Exotica's viewers, who are at first mystified by the discontinuity and gaps between scenes. Visibility, and the impact of the visual image, are central themes of this film. Voyeurism, Egoyan implies, is not only a humiliating position of someone who cannot achieve more, cannot have ‘the real thing’: it also forms a relationship, and allows a unique position of power. By watching attentively one can influence the story, transform its sequence.

Is that why Francis's wife attempts to cover his camcorder lens with her hand, when he videotapes her and their daughter?

The film opens at the airport, where customs inspectors watch the passengers through a one-way mirror; their gaze could determine a passenger's fate. We soon move to the ‘Exotica’ nightclub, where men watch the strippers, at times through a one-way mirror too. Francis (Bruce Greenwood) repeatedly watches a young stripper, Christina (Mia Kirshner), but their interaction appears highly ceremonial, built on set rules and steady mantras, which form a story co-constructed by the two of them. ‘What would happen if someone ever hurt you?’, he repeats; ‘You'll always be there to protect me’, she replies.

An additional central image is supplied by the pet shop owned by Thomas (Don McKellar), specialising in exotic animals. Seemingly, here we find the film's central motif: human beings as strange exotic animals, torn out of their natural habitat, forced to survive in an artificial aquarium-like reality, imitating their original milieu. ‘Just because they are exotic doesn't mean they can't endure extremes. It is, after all, a jungle out there, isn't it?’ says Francis, who comes as a tax inspector to check Thomas's books (once more, a penetrating fateful gaze). The Rousseau-styled artificial tropical vegetation of the ‘Exotica’ club portrays another such aquarium.

responseSet.title: “‘A Cure with a Defect”: A Previously Unpublished Letter by Freud Concerning “Anna O.”’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“‘A Cure with a Defect”

A Previously Unpublished Letter by Freud Concerning “Anna O.”’

Marian Tolpin

Marian Tolpin

Marian Tolpin  

Dear Sir,

John Forrester's and Laura Cameron's publication of Freud's unwonted description of Anna O's ‘cure with a defect’ (Int. J. Psychoanal., 80:929-42) is of particular interest to me because it accords with my interpretation of a ‘cure by compensatory structure’ (Tolpin, 1993)—i.e. the establishment of a circumscribed sector of the patient's self that is firmed up enough to sustain and support a meaningful, productive life. I want to briefly cite some of the new evidence for my interpretation (see Hirschmuller, 1989) because it also lends credence to the idea of the later health of Anna O. that Freud mentioned in answer to Tansley. This evidence enables us now, first, to make a reliable ‘reconstruction’ of the treatment and its traumatic ending; and, second, to make a critical reassessment of Anna O.

357

responseSet.title: The Cast of Characters: Paul Schwaber. New Haven, CT: Yale Univ. Press. 1999. Pp. 236 + xix.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Cast of Characters

Paul Schwaber. New Haven, CT: Yale Univ. Press. 1999. Pp. 236 + xix.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street, New York, NY 10028

Aaron Esman  

There is now a fair consensus that James Joyce's Ulysses (1922) is the most important literary work of the twentieth century. It has spawned a vast critical literature, dedicated, among other things, to clarifying some of its presumed obscurities, elucidating its parallels with its Homeric model and mapping the geography and the populace of the novel against the actualities of Dublin on ‘Bloomsday’—16 June, 1904.

Schwaber, a professor of English literature and a practising psychoanalyst, has chosen to address the novel through a study of the principal players in its unfolding drama—its ‘cast of characters’. Through a meticulous textual study, he focuses a psychoanalytic eye on these figures—primarily Leopold Bloom, the protagonist; Stephen Dedalus, its Telemachus; Molly Bloom, the Penelope; and some of the lesser personalities, such as Gerty McDowell, the Nausicaa, and The Citizen, its Cyclops.

Joyce's connections with Freud and psychoanalysis have been topics of considerable controversy. Although his ‘stream of consciousness’ technique appears to be related to Freudian free association, Joyce vehemently denied this; he insisted that he derived it from Edmond Dujardin's novel Les Lauriers Sont Coupés (Ellmann, 1982, p. 126; Svevo, 1927). He expressed considerable disdain for Freud and his concern about ‘the unconscious’ (Ellmann, 1982, p. 436, p. 546), and Italo Svevo1 maintains that when Joyce left Trieste in 1915 ‘he knew nothing about psychoanalysis’ (1927, n. p.). But Ellmann notes that Joyce

had in his possession in Trieste three small pamphlets in German, Freud's Leonardo essay, Ernest Jones's The Problem of Hamlet and the Oedipus Complex and Jung's The Significance of the Father in the Destiny of the Individual. They were all published in German between 1909 and 1911, and it seems likely that Joyce purchased them during that period. He probably heard about psychoanalysis from Ettore Schmitz’ [i.e. Italo Svevo!] (p. 340 n.)

In any event, it is hard to imagine anyone in the intellectual world in Trieste or anywhere else in the Austro-Hungarian empire who by 1915 ‘knew nothing about psychoanalysis’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Entre séduction et inspiration: l'homme [Man—between seduction and inspiration]

Jean Laplanche. Paris: Quadrige. Presses Universitaires de France. 1999. Pp. 338.

Elsa Schmid-Kitsikis

Elsa Schmid-Kitsikis

30, chemin de Conches, 1231 Conches
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA Cantab., MITI)

Elsa Schmid-Kitsikis  

This compendium of texts published by Jean Laplanche between 1992 and 1999 further develops the topics covered in New Foundations for Psychoanalysis (1987), in La révolution copernicienne inachevée [The unfinished Copernican revolution] (1992), which contains material published between 1967 and 1992 and in Fourvoiement biologisant de la sexualité chez Freud [How Freud led sexuality astray into biology] (1993).

Choosing to debate mostly with Freud but a little with Lacan, the author here displays the tenacity that was the hallmark of his previous books and continues his exploration of the role played in psychic development by the relationship of seduction, seen as the essence of the phenomenon of seduction. He describes this relationship in terms of activity—passivity, because the active protagonist is characterised by a greater unconscious ‘knowledge’, which is responsible for the emergence in the relationship of a specific entity, namely the enigmatic signifier. This he places at a deeper level than the limited vicissitudes of infantile seduction (the perverse father) and early seduction (maternal care), defining it as primal seduction in the context of his theory of generalised seduction (Laplanche's concepts are defined and discussed at length in Laplanche 1987, 1995, 1997).

The author's themes fall into two main groups. The first, which is the subject of Chapters 4, 7, 9 and 11, is ‘the ideological, institutional and political imperialism … of Freudism’, of which Lacanism is stated to be ‘merely one of the most accomplished manifestations’, as well as the institutional and training model of the French Psychoanalytical Association (APF), which is described as ‘freshly hatched from Lacanism’. In his examination of the effect of institutional ideologies on psychoanalysis, notions of filiation and transmission in regard to teaching, the place of psychoanalysis in the scientific community, its responsibility in the academic setting and its aims as a therapeutic process, Laplanche forcefully explains his rejection of the so-called training analysis and defends the conception of psychoanalysis as a science amenable to criticism, refutation and falsification in accordance with Popper's criteria for deeming a theory scientific.

responseSet.title: Finding Words: Affect, Behaviour and Meaning in the Psychoanalysis of Children
responseSet.authorMast: Martin Silverman and  Nancy R. Goodman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Finding Words

Affect, Behaviour and Meaning in the Psychoanalysis of Children

Martin Silverman and Nancy Goodman

Nancy Goodman

Maplewood, NJ

6917 Arlington Road, Suite 220
Bethesda
MD 20814

Nancy Goodman  

Child psychoanalysis provides a particularly rich terrain for the study of developmental progressions and regressions and the way in which movement towards adaptation tends to contain valiant attempts to survive painful and overwhelming affects. Peter Blos Jr, Chair of the Organising Committee, introduced this panel on child analysis, in which two case presentations and discussions were used to illustrate how psychoanalysts working with young children go about finding words for and giving meaning to affects and behaviour. The two case presenters, with exceptional ability, used rich process material and revealing discussion to portray the affective aliveness of the treatments.

In his introductory remarks, the Moderator, Martin Silverman, proposed listening to the cases with an ear to similarities and differences between the two children and the two analysts and to the question of what factors ultimately create effective treatment. He pointed out that both children tested the limits of their analysts' endurance and care. Both analysts proved to be patient, persistent and tolerant as they drew strength from their understanding of the defensive meaning of the children' words, play and actions. He addressed differences between Freudian and Kleinian approaches to the inner and outer worlds and that we are always working with what has occurred both in interaction with the environment and within the child' internal psychic representations and fantasies.

The first panellist, Liliana Pualuan de Gomberoff, presented a case entitled, ‘The fear of disappearing: affects aroused by separation as observed in the analysis of a 3-year-old girl’.

The 3-year-old Aurelia attended analysis four times a week, and entered it presenting a precocious and charming use of language. She was a little girl who acted like a ‘know-it-all’, became inconsolably sad and miserable at times, and seemed to make use of her wide vocabulary to avoid real contact with her mother, father and baby brother. Her analyst saw her as encased in her words and as closed to contact, as was the basket of toys that she would not open during initial sessions. During the first months of analysis, Liliana Pualuan de Gomberoff told her patient how she used words to stay away from feelings that were locked inside of her.

responseSet.authorCitation: Kernberg, O. F. & Ahumada, J. L.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion, A Binocular View

Groups and Individuals

Otto Kernberg and Jorge Ahumada

Jorge Ahumada

New York

Avenida Las Heras 3898, 30 H 1425 Buenos Aires

Jorge Ahumada  

The Moderator, Otto F. Kernberg, stressed Bion's growing influence, saying that although his concepts and findings had been subject at first to the splits of idealisation and rejection, now most schools used at least some of them. Thus, his ideas on group process permeated psychoanalytic understanding of groups in the United States, and Kernberg's own notions on severe personality disorders had been moulded, he said, by the technical ideas of Second Thoughts (1967) and Attention and Interpretation (1970).

Kernberg went on to say that when we focus on the ‘binocular view’ of psychic reality and external reality, on the unconscious group modality or group culture on the one hand and the conscious view-points, observations and actions of individuals in the group on the other, a dynamic unfolds between the collaborative function of the ‘work group’, as opposed to the unconscious activation of the basic assumptions group of ‘dependency’, ‘fight-flight’ and ‘pairing’, which, of course, has its equivalet dynamics at the individual level.

The ‘selected fact’, said Kernberg, was Bion's basic technical instrument, in groups as well as in psychoanalysis. He went beyond Fenichel's metapschology of interpretation, which was centred on three viewpoints: economic (the main instinctual investment and the dominant transference identification), dynamic (approaching the surface, the defence side, before focusing on the impulse) and structural (tending, in general, to formulate interpretations from the side of the observing ego). Bion approached the material ‘without memory or desire’, with no preset theoretical frameworks nor urges to influence the patient, the implication being that, in his listening the analyst lets himself be ‘invaded’ by the patient's non-verbal and verbal communications, and attends to the emotional developments and fantasies in his own experience in terms of the effects of the analysand's projective identification.

In working with small unstructured groups whose task was restricted to free and open communication among themselves for a limited time, Bion as group leader acted as a participant-observer, focusing on the emergence of a ‘selected fact’, which brings together, from a specific vertex, the multiplicity of manifestations. This method allowed him to describe three basic assumption groups: ‘dependency’, ‘fight-flight’ and ‘pairing’.

responseSet.title: Freud Comes to Palestine: A Study of Psychoanalysis in A Cultural Context
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud Comes to Palestine

A Study of Psychoanalysis in A Cultural Context

Alex Liban and Dodi Goldman

Dodi Goldman

Oranim, Israel
Dr Goldman
37 Nassau Drive
Great Neck
NY 11021

Dodi Goldman  

The authors describe the founding of Hashomer Hatzair as a radical Zionist scouting movement in Eastern Europe between 1913 and 1919, a little known episode in the rich history of Freud's impact upon this century. As refugees in Vienna, the young adherents of the movement experienced enormous personal and collective turmoil. Desperate to construct new, viable identities, these intellectually vibrant young men and women were drawn to Freud as part of their project of self-creation. Beginning in the 1920s, as members of Hashomer Hatzair settled in agriculturally based collectives known as kibbutzim, the educational leadership of the movement argued that psychoanalytically informed education was the key to raising children free of bourgeois neuroses. They established strong ties with European analysts, translated and published psychoanalytic texts, insisted that educators be analysed or, at least, psychoanalytically informed, and built a complex educational system founded on their particular understanding of Freudian insights. For them, psychoanalysis was also seen as a general prophylactic guaranteeing the mental hygiene of the community as a whole. The authors examine the complex relationship between Hashomer Hatzair and psychoanalysis. In particular, they ask why these young adults were so drawn to Freud and what their particular reading of the psychoanalytic texts was, and demonstrate how these young pioneers created a ‘usable Freud’ as part of their project of designing and building a utopian society.

responseSet.authorCitation: Auchincloss, E. L. & Kravis, N. M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Teaching Freud To Undergraduates

A Case Report

Elizabeth Auchincloss and Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

Dr Auchincloss

Nathan Kravis  

The authors describe their experience teaching ‘The Writings of Sigmund Freud’ to undergraduates at Columbia College over a three-year period. The course focused on Freud's developing theory of the mind, his application of psychoanalytic theory to the study of culture, and the autobiographical and historical context in which the texts were written. The students entered the course intrigued by the controversy surrounding Freud. They immersed themselves in the intellectual task of understanding Freud's work while at the same time discovering its power to speak directly to their personal concerns. The students found in Freud a companion in the search for personal meaning in the ‘great books’ of the western canon. At a deeper level, they found in Freud an ally in the drama of coming-of-age, experienced in terms of separation and loss, concerns about normality and sexuality, the quest for autonomy and the search for identity. While interested in Freud's ideas about infantile sexuality, for the most part, they rejected the concept of the Oedipus complex. The authors present excerpts from the students’written work and classroom discussion. Their responses are explored in relation to the developmental tasks of adolescence.

responseSet.title: Sándor Ferenczi: President of the International Psychoanalytical Association (1918-1920) and founder of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sándor Ferenczi

President of the International Psychoanalytical Association (1918-1920) and founder of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis

Ernst Falzeder and Judith Dupont

Judith Dupont

Dr Falzeder
Nr 1, A- 4582
 Spital am Pyhrn
Austria

Judith Dupont  

Dear Sir,

In his obituary of Karl Abraham, Ernest Jones wrote: ‘Blows of another sort have several times affected the psycho-analytical movement, one of which even brought with it the loss, by another way than death, of a President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association’ (1926, p. 155). As a matter of fact, before Abraham's death the IPA had ‘lost’ not one, but two presidents, so that we are left speculating about whom Jones was talking here. The wording, the context and subsequent events, however, leave little doubt that Jones was referring to Carl Gustav Jung, and that the Totschweigen of Sándor Ferenczi was already in full force.

In 1918, Ferenczi had been elected President of the IPA at the Fifth International Psychoanalytical Association Congress in Budapest. Due to the post-war upheaval and turmoil in Hungary, which made international contacts practically impossible, he had to ask someone else to act for him as President until the following congress, but he did not resign. The mere fact of his presidency has been all but forgotten, and it was only in 1996 that the former President of the IPA, Horacio Etchegoyen, noticed ‘a glaring omission in the portrait gallery of Presidents [in Broomhills]—Sándor Ferenczi’ (Bonomi, 1999, p. 507).

This is not the place to go into the reasons for the repression of the fact that the very founder of the International Psychoanalytical Association had also been its third President. Neither let us comment on the incidence that the person whom Ferenczi asked to act in his stead, and who also was the Editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, founded under Ferenczi's presidency, made no mention of him in its pages. The purpose of this brief communication is to draw attention to a hitherto overlooked document that helps to set much of the historical record straight. It is Ferenczi's ‘Open letter’, which opens the very first issue of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (1920), and cannot be found in the German, English, French or Spanish editions of Ferenczi's Collected Works. In it, Ferenczi points to his decision ‘to found a distinct Journal in the English language … as an official organ of the “International Psycho-Analytical Association”’, and to his appointment of Ernest Jones as Editor ‘to undertake this task’ (p. 1), and that he had not stepped down from the presidency, but had only asked Jones ‘to act for [him] as President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association until the next Congress’ (p. 2). And he signs in the very way in which he deserves to be remembered: ‘S. Ferenczi, MD, President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association’.
